args: An 86-year-old man was transferred by wheelchair from the skilled-nursing wing of a long-term care facility after staff noted two days of progressively deeper cough that turned purulent overnight and was now interspersed with brief, sharp stabs of right-sided pleuritic discomfort; he had refused the facility’s standing order for acetaminophen so that “the hospital can see the real numbers,” and the nurse escorted him in a private van without supplemental oxygen, nebulized therapy, intravenous fluids, or antibiotics. His chart from the home lists hypertension controlled with amlodipine 5 mg daily and remote adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon resected six years ago followed by adjuvant capecitabine, with no evidence of recurrence on surveillance CT performed five months earlier; there is no documentation of chronic kidney impairment, cirrhosis, or heart muscle dysfunction, and his most recent metabolic panel three weeks ago showed a creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL. He quit a 20-pack-year cigarette habit three decades ago, drinks only tea, and has never used illicit substances. Immunization records at the facility confirm both pneumococcal series and this season’s inactivated influenza vaccine. Upon stretcher arrival he sat upright, breathing through pursed lips and clutching his right flank with each cough; mucous membranes looked dry, skin was hot and flushed, and capillary refill was under two seconds. Untreated triage vitals on ambient air at this sea-level institution were: oral temperature 102.6 °F, irregularly regular radial pulse 137 beats per minute confirmed on telemetry as atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, automated right-arm blood pressure 148/76 mm Hg corroborated by manual measurement, respiratory rate 36 breaths per minute with mild supraclavicular retraction, and pulse oximetry 94 % on room air. Neck veins were not distended, and no peripheral edema was appreciated. Cardiac auscultation revealed a fast S1 and S2 with variable intensity, no murmurs or extra sounds. Pulmonary examination demonstrated absent breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the lower half of the right posterior thorax with egophony superiorly; scattered coarse crackles peppered the remaining fields, left greater than right. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender, with a well-healed midline laparotomy scar. A finger-stick glucose obtained before vascular access read 146 mg/dL. Venous chemistry drawn within ten minutes—before any saline flush—returned sodium 168 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, chloride 112 mEq/L, bicarbonate 26 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 64 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.0 mg/dL. Complete blood count showed leukocytes 18.9 × 10⁹/L with 89 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 58 %, hemoglobin 19.1 g/dL, and platelets 220 × 10⁹/L. A right radial arterial sample on room air demonstrated pH 7.71, PaCO₂ 23 mm Hg, PaO₂ 102 mm Hg, and calculated bicarbonate 29 mEq/L, consistent with marked respiratory alkalosis with mild hypoxemia. Serum lactate was 2.0 mmol/L, high-sensitivity troponin fell below assay detection limits, and NT-proBNP was 190 pg/mL (similar to a value on file from his last outpatient visit). A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph obtained fifteen minutes after arrival—prior to any therapeutic intervention—revealed dense consolidation occupying the right middle and lower lobes accompanied by a concave superior meniscus that obliterated the right costophrenic angle, compatible with a free-flowing effusion; the cardiac silhouette was within normal limits for age, mediastinal contours were unchanged from prior imaging, and lung volumes were slightly reduced. Point-of-care thoracic ultrasound confirmed an anechoic layer measuring approximately 2.5 cm in depth posteriorly and demonstrated dynamic air bronchograms in the adjacent parenchyma. Twelve-lead electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 136 bpm, nonspecific ST-T wave changes, and no acute ischemic pattern when compared to a tracing from the nursing facility two months prior. Two peripheral sets of blood cultures, an induced sputum specimen for Gram stain and culture, urinary Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella antigens, and a multiplex viral PCR panel were sent before empiric antimicrobials or other agents were ordered. As this note is entered he remains seated upright on 21 % inspired oxygen, has not yet received intravenous crystalloid, bronchodilators, antipyretics, diuretics, corticosteroids, or parenteral antibiotics, thereby preserving all vital signs, laboratory results, and imaging findings above as his untreated presentation physiology.

args: A 65-year-old man with a background of poorly controlled hypertension, permanent atrial fibrillation on long-term apixaban, and moderate chronic obstructive pulmonary disease from forty pack-years of smoking was discovered by a neighbour at 04:45 lying prone at the foot of the apartment stairwell after an apparent fall from the third-floor landing; there was a shallow pool of vomitus beside him, and he was snoring with irregular respirations but made no purposeful movements. Emergency medical technicians arrived seven minutes later and noted pinpoint but reactive pupils, a heart rate of 104 beats per minute in an irregularly irregular rhythm, and a palpable radial pulse corresponding to an automated cuff reading of 124/78 mm Hg. Because he had copious oral secretions, a Glasgow Coma assessment in the field yielded eye opening only to painful trapezius squeeze, incomprehensible gurgling sounds, and decerebrate extension of both upper limbs, summating 6 overall, the crew proceeded with rapid-sequence orotracheal intubation using 30 mg etomidate and 80 mg rocuronium. A 7.5-mm cuffed tube was placed on the first attempt and secured at 23 cm at the incisors; lung auscultation confirmed equal breath sounds and capnography displayed appropriate waveform. He was connected to a transport ventilator set to volume assist-control mode delivering a tidal volume of 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, a mandatory rate of 16, 5 cm H₂O of positive end-expiratory pressure, and an inspired oxygen fraction of 31 %. No crystalloid bolus, vasopressor infusion, anticonvulsant, antifibrinolytic, or sedative drip was administered during the eleven-minute transport. On arrival in the resuscitation area—well before any hospital-initiated therapy—the patient remained on the same ventilator settings, and a right radial arterial catheter placed within two minutes showed blood pressure 121/71 mm Hg with a mean of 88 mm Hg, confirming the absence of circulatory collapse. Pulse oximetry fluctuated between 96 % and 97 % on the documented 31 % inspired oxygen. The ventilator displayed peak inspiratory pressures of 22 cm H₂O and plateau 17 cm H₂O with a tidal volume of 460 mL. An arterial blood gas drawn simultaneously revealed pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 19 mEq/L, and PaO₂ 111 mm Hg; lactate measured 2.3 mmol/L. Neurologic evaluation, performed during the brief window before a continuous propofol infusion was started, reproduced the field findings: eyes opened only to nail-bed pressure, guttural vowel sounds were emitted around the tube but no words, and the patient exhibited bilateral arm extension with internal rotation to central pain, maintaining the Glasgow Coma Score at 6. Pupils were 2.5 mm bilaterally and briskly reactive, corneal reflexes were present, cough reflex was diminished, and there was no lateralizing motor asymmetry. Scalp inspection revealed a 4-cm occipital laceration oozing venous blood but no obvious skull depression. Cardiovascular examination disclosed an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs, normal S₁/S₂, and jugular venous pulsation at 5 cm above the sternal angle while the patient was supine at 30°. Lung fields were clear to auscultation with symmetrical chest rise around the endotracheal tube, and the abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender. Skin was warm with capillary refill under two seconds, and there was no mottling or petechiae. A 16-French Foley catheter placed during secondary survey drained 85 mL of clear yellow urine immediately; a review of the patient’s home health aide log, retrieved from the mobile application the aide uses, indicated total voided volume of 1 330 mL in the previous twenty-four hours, consistent with preserved renal perfusion. Initial laboratory studies drawn concurrently with the arterial sample—before any intravenous fluids, reversal agents for apixaban, hyperosmolar therapy, or anticonvulsants—showed white blood cell count 11.4 ×10³/µL, hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, and platelet count 182 000 /µL. Comprehensive metabolic panel returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, anion gap 13, glucose 142 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 26 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.22 mg/dL, only slightly above a documented baseline of 1.1 mg/dL from a clinic visit two months prior. Total bilirubin measured 0.88 mg/dL with indirect predominance; aspartate aminotransferase 34 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 29 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 102 U/L, and international normalized ratio (reflecting apixaban rather than warfarin use) was 1.1. Serum magnesium and calcium were within normal limits, high-sensitivity troponin was undetectable, and C-reactive protein measured 6 mg/L. Non-contrast computed tomography of the head—obtained within fifteen minutes but prior to any hypertonic saline, mannitol, or reversal agent—demonstrated a right frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage measuring 18 mm with 2 mm of midline shift but no hydrocephalus; cervical spine CT was negative for fracture. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph performed while the patient remained on the initial ventilator configuration confirmed the endotracheal tube tip 2 cm above the carina, clear lung fields, and no rib fractures. Focused cardiac ultrasound revealed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction without regional wall-motion abnormality and a collapsible inferior vena cava measuring 1.6 cm. Up to the moment all the above physiologic and biochemical data were captured, the patient had received neither osmotherapy nor blood-pressure-modifying agents, remained hemodynamically stable at 121/71 mm Hg without pharmacologic support, was intubated and ventilated on 31 % inspired oxygen yielding an arterial oxygen tension of 111 mm Hg, possessed a platelet count of 182 000 per microliter, displayed essentially normal bilirubin and creatinine values with an adequate 24-hour urine output of 1 330 mL, and manifested a severely depressed level of consciousness quantified by a Glasgow Coma Score of 6 in the setting of acute traumatic intracerebral hemorrhage.

args: A 58-year-old woman with a decade-long history of non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (most recent transthoracic echocardiogram four months earlier showing a left-ventricular ejection fraction of 28 %) and chronic New York Heart Association class II–III dyspnea arrived at the emergency department at 14:10 after experiencing two episodes of forceful vomiting that filled a large plastic kitchen bowl with clotted, maroon-colored fluid and streaks of brighter red. She had been resting upright in a recliner after lunch when a sudden wave of nausea prompted her to hurry to the sink, where she retched repeatedly but never lost awareness, never felt the room spin, and never collapsed; she remained able to call a neighbor for a ride. Over the preceding week she had taken ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for bursitic shoulder pain and had skipped the evening dose of her usual omeprazole in order to “save pills until payday.” Her guideline-directed heart-failure regimen includes carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sacubitril–valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg each morning with an extra 20 mg “as needed” for weight gain, spironolactone 25 mg nightly, and a low-dose long-acting nitrate; there are no antiplatelet agents, anticoagulants, or corticosteroids in her pillbox. Baseline functional capacity allows her to climb half a flight of stairs before stopping to catch her breath, and she usually sleeps with two pillows; she denies any recent increase in orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or ankle swelling beyond her chronic trace edema. She has never been told she has viral hepatitis, cholestatic disease, or abnormal liver blood tests, has no history of alcohol misuse, and her most recent outpatient metabolic panel three months prior was unremarkable apart from stable mild hyponatremia. She quit smoking fifteen years ago after a twelve-pack-year exposure, drinks herbal tea instead of alcohol, and has never used illicit substances. Family history is notable for a father who died of congestive heart failure and a mother with type 2 diabetes; there are no known bleeding disorders. Review of systems obtained in triage is negative for fever, chills, cough, chest pain beyond her usual exertional pressure, exertional wheeze, black tarry stool, bright-red rectal bleeding, jaundice, pruritus, easy bruising, urinary color change, unintended weight loss, dizziness, or syncope. On arrival—prior to the placement of any intravenous catheter, administration of crystalloid, blood products, vasoactive drugs, antiemetics, or acid suppressive medications—she sat leaning forward on the stretcher, pale but conversant in full sentences. Manually obtained vital signs with the right arm supported at heart level showed a systolic blood pressure of 102 mm Hg and diastolic of 59 mm Hg; a three-lead cardiac monitor displayed a regular sinus pulse of 136 beats per minute; respirations were 24 per minute; oral temperature 36.6 °C; and oxygen saturation 96 % on room air. Capillary refill was approximately four seconds, conjunctivae were lightly blanched, sclerae anicteric, and oral mucous membranes were dry with streaks of old coffee-ground material coating the posterior pharynx. Neck veins were mildly distended halfway up the sternocleidomastoid at 45 °, and peripheral pulses were rapid yet thready. Cardiac auscultation revealed a tachycardic S1 and S2 with an audible S3 gallop but no new murmurs or rubs. Lung fields demonstrated faint bibasilar crackles that cleared with coughing, consistent with her chronic fluid status. The abdomen was scaphoid and soft; gentle deep palpation caused mild, diffuse epigastric discomfort without guarding, rebound, masses, or organomegaly, and there was no visible distention or ascites. Bowel sounds were hyperactive. Examination of the extremities showed trace bilateral ankle pitting edema and cool fingertips. A focused neurologic screen revealed an alert individual oriented to person, place, and time, with 5/5 strength in all extremities and intact finger-to-nose testing; gait assessment was deferred owing to active monitoring. After verbal consent, a single 18-gauge catheter was placed in the left antecubital fossa solely for diagnostic sampling, and before any therapeutic infusion the initial laboratory panel returned: hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, hematocrit 36 %, mean corpuscular volume 87 fL, platelet count 226 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 10.1 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 70 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.3 mg/dL), sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, and international normalized ratio 1.12. A 12-lead electrocardiogram recorded simultaneously demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 138 bpm with nonspecific ST-T changes but no acute ischemia. Upright chest radiography displayed borderline cardiomegaly with mild pulmonary vascular congestion and no focal infiltrates; a single-view abdominal film showed a nonspecific gas pattern without free air. Rectal examination was deferred initially to avoid provoking further nausea. At this earliest snapshot she remains nil per os, attached to continuous cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring while type and screen processing is underway, and gastroenterology has been urgently paged for esophagogastroduodenoscopy to locate and control the presumed proximal source of her repeated hematemesis in the setting of reduced cardiac reserve.

args:
The patient is a 35-year-old lady presented to the primary care physician with the chief complaint of cough. Her past medical history is significant for a possible tuberculosis infection 15 years ago. She was in direct contact with another patient who was positive for tuberculosis at a gathering event and later completed a six-month course of anti-tuberculosis regimen prescribed by her primary care physician. She otherwise remained to be asymptomatic and healthy. Her nonproductive cough began three weeks ago. She denied fever, chest pain, dyspnea, night sweats, and weight loss but did complain of sore throat and occasional chills. She lived at home with her family and reported to have sick contact with her kids as they were suffering from an upper respiratory infection. She decided to present to the office for further evaluation due to concerns about possible tuberculosis reactivation.
Her initial vital signs on presentation were as follows: blood pressure 116/59 mmHg, heart rate 81 beats per minute, temperature 97.8°F, body mass index 20.97. Physical examination was completely benign. There was a low clinical suspicion of active tuberculosis given her overall clinical impression. She was recommended to follow up in the clinic in a few weeks after obtaining a two-view chest x-ray (CXR), sputum acid-fast bacillus (AFB) smear, and culture. The patient was followed up via telemedicine over the next few weeks. She continued to have a mild cough with a low clinical suspicion for tuberculosis. The AFB smear and culture could not have been completed due to a lack of sputum samples. CXR revealed small rim calcified lesions in the right mid and upper lung zones suggestive of a benign etiology without findings of active or latent tuberculosis infection (Figure ). The patient was recommended to schedule a follow-up appointment in six months while maintaining a close observation of her symptoms.
Six months later, her cough reoccurred after an initial improvement over the past few months. The cough was accompanied by occasional sputum. She denied fever, chills, night sweat, sore throat, chest pain, dyspnea, or weight loss. She had been in close contact with people who were diagnosed with an upper respiratory infection. She was recommended to undergo a pulmonary function test (PFT) as well as a chest CT for further evaluation. She was prescribed dextromethorphan for symptomatic relief, educated on the symptoms of active tuberculosis, and instructed to give frequent updates of her symptoms. CT with intravenous contrast revealed a high right paratracheal and medial right apical heterogeneous soft tissue with central areas of calcification (Figure ). She agreed to opt for a routine endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA) to rule out malignancy.
Bronchoscopy inspection revealed unremarkable trachea, left upper lobe, left lingula, left lower lobe, right middle lobe, and right lower lobe; but interestingly, no right upper lobe was observed. Furthermore, EBUS-TBNA showed a right paratracheal mass without surrounding lymphadenopathies that was biopsied and sent to pathology for analysis. The presumptive diagnosis for the right paratracheal mass was likely due to a nondeveloped right upper lung with associated scarring and atelectasis. The pathology report later confirmed the finding as there was no indication of malignancy or fungal infection.

args:
A 57-year-old white man presented with a 3-month history of new-onset, severe, bi-frontal headaches and a 13.6 kg (30-lb) weight loss. Local emergency room evaluation revealed a negative computed tomography (CT) scan of his head. Laboratory findings included an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 80 mm/hour and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 55.3 mg/L. He was discharged with pain control.
During follow-up with his local primary provider, his headache persisted and bilateral jaw pain and left facial numbness had developed. He was started on prednisone 60 mg/day for presumed GCA with partial improvement in his headache. Bilateral temporal artery biopsies were performed 3 days later and were negative. Prednisone was subsequently discontinued. Two weeks later his headache progressed and right eye horizontal diplopia and perioral numbness developed. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of his brain was performed and interpreted as normal. He was admitted for pulse-dose steroids (1000 mg daily for 3 days) which led to resolution of visual symptoms and was discharged on prednisone 60 mg/day. Tapering below 50 mg/day was unsuccessful due to rising inflammatory markers, symptom progression, and return of diplopia. Severe left hip pain developed for which plain radiographs were obtained but negative for fracture or avascular necrosis.
On referral to our institution he continued to have ongoing headache and left jaw numbness despite 6 weeks of high-dose glucocorticoids (80 mg/day). Left hip pain had worsened to the point of wheelchair dependency. His past medical history was remarkable for atrial fibrillation for which he was receiving warfarin 2 mg/day and diltiazem 240 mg/day. Social history was notable for lack of tobacco or alcohol use and absence of known environmental exposures during his employment as an office manager. Aside from prostate cancer in his father, our patient’s family history was negative for other pertinent diagnoses of additional malignancies or autoimmune conditions. Laboratory evaluation noted an ESR 72 mm/hour, CRP 53 mg/L, hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, leukocytes 5.2 × 109/L and platelets 110 × 109/L, international normalized ratio (INR) 2.3, creatinine 1.04 mg/dL, calcium 11.6 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 106 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 64 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 360 U/L, total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL, total protein 5.1 g/dL, and albumin 3.4 g/dL. Previously obtained autoimmune serologies were negative for antinuclear and extractable nuclear antigens as well as rheumatoid factor, anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide, myeloperoxidase, and proteinase-3 antibodies.
His vital signs demonstrated a heart rate of 95 beats per minute, blood pressure of 94/44 mm Hg, respiratory rate of 12 per minute, oxygenation saturation of 96%, and weight of 144 kg with body mass index (BMI) of 48.6 kg/m2. An examination was notable for a Cushingoid appearance and morbid obesity. Evaluation of adenopathy and splenomegaly was limited due to body habitus. Pertinent cardiovascular findings included irregularly irregular pulse without murmurs. Upper and lower arterial pulses were normal and symmetric. Common superficial temporal artery palpation was without tenderness or nodularity. Trace pitting edema was noted to the ankle bilaterally. Breath sounds were equal and symmetric without wheezing or rhonchi. Aside from scattered bruising at site of venipuncture, no cutaneous abnormalities were noted. A neurologic examination showed normal speech without language deficits. Visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally. Cranial nerves 2–12 were assessed and normal except for mild esotropia of his right eye and hypoesthesia to light touch and pinprick over the left mandibular region of the left trigeminal nerve. The remaining dermatomes evaluated showed normal sensation. Reflexes were normal and symmetric throughout and toes were down-going bilaterally. No ataxia was observed. A musculoskeletal examination demonstrated normal range of motion of upper extremities without deficit. Marked pain was noted on passive and active range of motion of his left hip.
Given the persistent headache despite high-dose glucocorticoids and atypical features, alternative etiologies were suspected. The local MRI of his brain was reviewed and evidence of abnormal enhancing soft tissue involving Meckel’s cave bilaterally with extension through the foramen ovale was noted (Fig. a). The differential diagnosis for infiltrative process in Meckel’s cave included sarcoidosis as well as primary or secondary neoplastic lesions such as meningioma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, schwannoma, neurofibroma, and lymphoma [, ]. The presence of constitutional symptoms and elevated inflammatory markers suggested a secondary process with associated intracranial involvement. As such, positron emission tomography (PET)-CT was obtained. This demonstrated extensive hypermetabolic lesions throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton, including the skull base, as well as fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-avid lymph nodes above and below the diaphragm (Fig. c). MRI of his left hip revealed diffusely abnormal marrow signal suggestive of infiltrative disease. A cervical lymph node biopsy demonstrated evidence of diffuse large B cell lymphoma and staging bone marrow biopsy revealed a hypercellular marrow (90%) with diffuse large B cell lymphoma involving 70% of the total cellularity (Fig. ). Initiation of high-dose methotrexate (3.5 g/m2), rituximab (375 mg/m2), cyclophosphamide (750 mg/m2), hydroxydaunorubicin (50 mg/m2), Oncovin (vincristine; 2 mg), and prednisone (255 mg) (MR-CHOP; 21-day cycle) resulted in interval improvement in his headache and visual symptoms (Fig. b, d).
Ten months after initial diagnosis and 4 months after completion of his sixth cycle of MR-CHOP, our patient re-presented with bilateral lower extremity weakness and PET-CT showed recurrence of disease with evidence of neurolymphomatosis of multiple cervical, thoracic, and sacral nerve roots. Intrathecal cytarabine (50 mg) was given and ibrutinib (140 mg/day) was initiated, resulting in initial findings of improved strength and subsequent decreased nerve root FDG-avidity.

args: A 44-year-old man presented to the hospital-based anticoagulation service for an unscheduled consultation after the community laboratory faxed two consecutive supratherapeutic prothrombin time results earlier in the week. Since initiation he has attended standing weekly draws, yet printed logs brought to clinic today show marked fluctuation: during the most recent eight-week stretch values have spanned from 1.6, 2.8, 2.2, 3.3, 4.1, 1.7, 3.9, to 4.5, necessitating several telephone dose adjustments that alternate 5-mg and 7.5-mg tablets and the occasional hold. No hospitalization, vitamin K administration, plasma, or prothrombin complex concentrate has been required, and he denies ever passing dark or bloody stools, vomiting blood, coughing up blood, gross hematuria, spontaneous epistaxis, gingival oozing, large ecchymoses, or needing transfusion. His chief concern is whether the “roller-coaster” blood tests, the combination of tablets he takes for his coronary history, and the amount of alcohol he consumes each week could predispose him to a catastrophic hemorrhage. Myocardial infarction at age 40 led to the placement of a drug-eluting stent in the proximal left anterior descending artery, after which his internist prescribed chewable aspirin 81 mg daily for secondary prevention, a regimen he continues. Intermittent anterior knee pain from an old basketball injury prompts him to self-treat with over-the-counter ibuprofen 400 mg one to two times most evenings after work; in addition, his primary clinician started sertraline 50 mg each morning two years ago to manage generalized anxiety related to job strain. He has never been told he has chronically elevated blood pressure; home wrist-cuff readings that he dutifully records twice a week are typically in the 110s to low 120s systolic, and three manual measurements performed after five minutes of seated rest in clinic average 118/74 mm Hg with an irregularly irregular pulse of 84 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air. He denies yellowing of the eyes, pruritus, right-upper-quadrant discomfort, abdominal distention, confusion, or prior abnormal liver enzyme panels. A comprehensive metabolic profile drawn three days earlier lists sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 13 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 95 mL/min/1.73 m², aspartate aminotransferase 26 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, and albumin 4.3 g/dL. Point-of-care hemoglobin in clinic today is 14.2 g/dL, platelet count 238 × 10⁹/L, and the finger-stick INR performed prior to any dosing reads 4.2. A 12-lead electrocardiogram confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 78 beats/min, normal axis, and no acute ischemic changes; a transthoracic echocardiogram from last month demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 58 %, mild left atrial enlargement, and no significant valvular pathology. He never smoked cigarettes and denies illicit drug use, but his alcohol intake is substantial and consistent: two 12-ounce beers with dinner most weeknights, three additional beers while watching sports on each weekend day, and mixed drinks—typically two bourbon-colas—at weekly darts league on Thursday, totaling approximately 29 alcoholic beverages per week; he reports no morning tremor, early-day drinking, blackouts, or legal issues but admits occasional sluggishness the following morning. args: A 35-year-old man with no documented medical conditions and no prescription medications was air-lifted from a commercial construction site after co-workers witnessed him topple backward off the second-story decking and land supine on packed dirt; they report a brief gasp followed by silence, with neither limb convulsions nor vomitus observed, and they applied no first aid beyond maintaining cervical alignment with their hands until paramedics arrived nine minutes later. Flight crew placed a rigid collar, log-rolled him onto a vacuum mattress, inserted a single 18-gauge catheter into the right antecubital vein that was flushed only with 5 mL saline, and delivered 4 L/min oxygen by nasal cannula because respirations were spontaneous but shallow; they administered no crystalloids, opioids, benzodiazepines, paralytics, antiemetics, dextrose, or vasoactive agents during the seventeen-minute transport. On touchdown he was transferred directly to the trauma bay still secured in full spinal precautions, clothing caked with red clay but free of blood beyond a 2-cm oozing laceration behind the left auricle. Initial pre-intervention vitals obtained concurrently with the first head-to-toe assessment showed blood pressure 138/82 mm Hg in the left brachial artery, heart rate 94 beats/min in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min with symmetrical chest excursion, oxygen saturation 95 % on the same nasal cannula, and core temperature 37.0 °C via esophageal probe placed by flight crew. Airway exam revealed intact dentition, a midline tongue, and copious gravel dust lining the gingiva yet no pooled secretions; a Yankauer tip touching the soft palate provoked a robust gag and two-second cough. Breath sounds were vesicular and equal, percussion resonant, and there was no accessory muscle use. Cardiac auscultation produced a crisp S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; radial and dorsalis pedis pulses were 2+ bilaterally and capillary refill was under two seconds. Abdomen was flat, soft, and non-tender; pelvis was stable to gentle compression. Long-bone survey identified mild swelling over the right forearm without crepitus or deformity; distal pulses and motor function in all extremities were present. During a silent forty-second observation the patient lay motionless with eyelids fully shut; lashes did not flutter and there was no spontaneous ocular movement. Repeated calling of his first name at conversational and then shout-level volume failed to elicit any reaction. When a firm supra-orbital notch pressure was applied for five seconds, both lids suddenly parted to mid-palpebral height and pupils, measuring 4 mm, rotated toward the examiner before drifting superiorly once the stimulus stopped, after which the lids slowly closed again. A similar response occurred with trapezius squeeze but not in the absence of noxious input. Speech was absent at rest; however, upon nail-bed compression of the left index finger the patient blurted clearly articulated but disconnected phrases—“blue ladder… lunchtime… where’s the drill”—that bore no relevance to questions about his name, location, or the event, and he ceased phonating immediately after the pain stopped. Cranial nerve testing was otherwise notable for brisk corneal reflexes, midline uvula, and symmetric facial grimace when the mandibular condyle was pressed. Muscle tone at rest was normal. When the same nail-bed stimulus was applied to the right thumb, the contralateral (left) hand lifted off the mattress, swept across midline, and purposefully attempted to grasp and push away the examiner’s fingers; the movement was accurate and terminated once the hand made contact, after which the limb settled back to the stretcher without abnormal posturing. An identical directed reach occurred toward a trapezius pinch on either side. Lower extremities displayed only mild hip flexion but no extension or withdrawal during these tests. Deep tendon reflexes were 2+ at biceps and patellae, 1+ at Achilles; plantar stimulation produced flexor responses bilaterally. No clonus was present. Skin examination revealed scattered superficial abrasions over both scapulae and a 3 × 4 cm contusion on the right occiput without palpable step-off. Portable anterior-posterior chest and pelvic radiographs completed within five minutes were unremarkable, and a focused assessment with sonography for trauma was negative for free fluid. Point-of-care venous blood gas prior to any medication administration showed pH 7.38, pCO₂ 38 mm Hg, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, and lactate 1.6 mmol/L; finger-stick glucose measured 108 mg/dL. No intravenous fluids, sedatives, analgesics, hyperosmolar therapy, or paralytics have been given since arrival, ensuring that the ocular, verbal, and motor behaviors detailed above reflect his untreated neurologic baseline. Neurosurgery, orthopedic trauma, and critical care teams have been paged, and stat non-contrast head and cervical spine CT along with CT angiography of the cervical vessels are being arranged while continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring and fifteen-minute neurologic checks proceed at bedside.

args: An 82-year-old right-hand-dominant man, a retired postal clerk who moved into his eldest son’s suburban home two winters ago after becoming increasingly forgetful about turning off the stovetop, was brought to the outpatient internal-medicine office this morning for a comprehensive pre-operative assessment prior to elective cataract extraction scheduled next month. According to the son, the patient remains physically active—he ambles around the backyard vegetable plot with a hand cultivator and can still manage a slow one-kilometre stroll to the neighbourhood pond—yet over the past three years he has required growing assistance with finances, medication organisation, and driving; on today’s Mini-Cog he recalled none of three words after five minutes and needed prompting to draw clock hands to “10 after 11,” though he was pleasant, oriented to year and city, and followed two-step commands without difficulty. He denies ever awakening with crushing chest discomfort, has never undergone coronary angiography, and serial troponins drawn during two separate emergency visits for influenza-related dehydration five and eight years ago remained below assay detection, suggesting no prior myocardial injury. He also reports no history of ankle swelling, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, or cardiologist follow-up, and a transthoracic echocardiogram done eighteen months earlier for a systolic murmur revealed normal left-ventricular size, an ejection fraction of 63 %, and only trace aortic sclerosis. Neurologic review is negative for episodes of unilateral weakness, facial droop, slurred speech, or visual field loss, and gait is steady with a slightly stooped posture but without assistive devices; motor strength today is 5/5 throughout, rapid alternating movements are intact, and plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. Respiratory history is similarly benign: he has never smoked, requires no inhalers, and a chest radiograph obtained last November for chronic cough showed clear lung fields with normal diaphragm contour. Endocrine records reveal type 2 diabetes diagnosed nine years ago after two fasting plasma glucose values of 142 mg/dL; he adheres to metformin 1000 mg twice daily and a carbohydrate-restricted plate model devised with a dietitian. Finger-stick logs provided by his son display pre-breakfast readings ranging 96–154 mg/dL, and last week’s glycated haemoglobin was 7.1 %. He reports intact vibratory sensation in the toes, denies intermittent claudication or foot ulceration, and a dilated retinal examination six months ago showed background retinopathy absent. Renal indices, however, have shown progressive decline: serum creatinine has risen from 1.4 mg/dL four years ago to 2.6 mg/dL today, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 26 mL/min/1.73 m²; blood urea nitrogen is 41 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mmol/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, and spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio last measured at 420 mg/g. He has not experienced volume overload, uremic pruritus, or pericarditic pain, but nephrology has discussed potential fistula planning should kidney function continue to deteriorate. Hepatic enzymes have been mildly abnormal since a life-insurance evaluation two decades ago: current alanine aminotransferase 58 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 62 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, albumin 3.9 g/dL, and international normalised ratio 1.1. An abdominal ultrasound performed last summer demonstrated a slightly echogenic but smooth-contoured liver measuring 14 cm with patent portal flow, no splenomegaly, and no ascites; he denies jaundice, telangiectasias, encephalopathic confusion beyond his baseline forgetfulness, abdominal distention, or gastrointestinal bleeding, and consumes at most one glass of red wine on holidays. He has no past epigastric pain, haematemesis, or melena and routinely avoids non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in favour of acetaminophen for arthritic knees. Current medications include metformin as noted, lisinopril 10 mg daily for diabetic nephropathy and blood-pressure readings that hover around 132/68 mm Hg, low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily initiated by his primary physician for vascular prophylaxis, vitamin D3 1000 IU, and a nightly over-the-counter magnesium citrate for constipation; he lists a remote rash to sulfonamide antibiotics and no other allergies. This morning’s vitals read: temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 136/70 mm Hg seated right arm, heart rate 72 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air, and body-mass index 24 kg/m². He appears well nourished, speaks in full sentences, and is cooperative though intermittently repeats questions already answered. Neck is supple without carotid bruits, jugular venous pulsation is 3 cm above the sternal angle at 45°, and the point of maximal impulse lies in the fifth intercostal space mid-clavicular line; S1 and S2 are distinct with a soft midsystolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border, no gallops or pericardial rub. Lungs are clear to auscultation with vesicular breath sounds throughout and no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen is flat, bowel sounds normoactive, liver edge smooth and palpable 1 cm below the costal margin, spleen not felt, and there is no fluid wave. Extremities are warm with 2+ radial, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses, capillary refill under two seconds, and no oedema or skin discoloration. Skin shows scattered solar lentigines but no spider angiomas or palmar erythema. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats/min, PR interval 160 ms, QRS 92 ms, and no pathological Q waves or ST-segment deviation. The patient and son request clarification on peri-operative risks in the context of diminished renal reserve and strategies to ensure medication adherence given his memory lapses, as well as advice regarding low-protein diets their neighbour recommended for kidney protection.

args: A 67-year-old man with a background of long-standing type 2 diabetes controlled on metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, and moderate chronic kidney disease (clinic creatinine 1.4 mg/dL six months earlier) was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his spouse noticed progressive swelling, erythema, and serosanguinous drainage from a chronic callus on the dorsal surface of his right great toe that had worsened over four days and was now accompanied by subjective fevers and rigors. According to the pre-hospital record, he was seated upright on first contact, speaking in complete sentences without shortness of breath, and no airway adjunct, intravenous fluid, antibiotic, or vasopressor was given in transit; the only intervention was placement of a blue-coded Venturi mask selected by the crew to deliver 0.22 fraction of inspired oxygen because the initial pulse oximetry reading on ambient air hovered in the high eighties. Ground transport time was eleven minutes. Upon stretcher entry to the resuscitation bay—and prior to any hospital-initiated therapy—vital signs obtained via an automatic cuff and confirmed immediately with a right radial arterial catheter showed blood pressure 131/79 mm Hg (mean 96 mm Hg), heart rate 104 beats min⁻¹ in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 20 breaths min⁻¹, temperature 38.3 °C oral, and oxygen saturation 95 % while he continued to breathe through the Venturi device at the documented 22 % FiO₂. He sat forward, anxious about his foot, but remained fully oriented to person, place, time, and circumstance, opening his eyes spontaneously, conversing appropriately, and obeying all commands—yielding a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 before the administration of any analgesic or sedative. Skin examination disclosed a 6-cm violaceous patch with indurated borders extending from the hallux to the first metatarsal head, purulent exudate visible at a central fissure, and surrounding warmth; no crepitus or bullae were present. Capillary refill at the fingers was two seconds, and peripheral pulses were easily palpable. Cardiac auscultation revealed a normal S₁/S₂ without murmurs; the jugular venous column was not elevated. Lung fields were clear to auscultation with equal excursion, and he generated tidal volumes comfortably without accessory-muscle use. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender, bowel sounds normoactive. A 16-French Foley catheter inserted for strict intake-output monitoring produced 90 mL of straw-colored urine on placement; review of home glucose-log entries, in which the patient also records fluid balance for his nephrologist, indicated approximately 1 480 mL of urine during the preceding twenty-four hours. An arterial blood gas drawn three minutes after line placement, while the Venturi mask remained at the same setting, returned pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, lactate 2.0 mmol/L, and PaO₂ 117 mm Hg on the stated 0.22 inspired oxygen. Concurrent laboratory studies—obtained before any crystalloids or antimicrobials—demonstrated sodium 133 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, glucose 213 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 34 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.62 mg/dL, slightly above his outpatient baseline. Complete blood count showed leukocytes 14.9 ×10³/µL with 11 % band forms, hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit 38 %, and a platelet concentration of 150 500 /µL. Liver chemistry panel revealed aspartate aminotransferase 46 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 41 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L, international normalized ratio 1.1, and a total bilirubin of 0.92 mg/dL. High-sensitivity troponin measured <0.01 ng/mL. A portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained while he remained on the low-flow Venturi mask showed clear lung fields and normal mediastinal contours. Point-of-care ultrasound demonstrated a small but collapsible inferior vena cava, preserved left-ventricular systolic function, and no pericardial effusion; focused assessment of the right foot revealed subcutaneous edema without gas-producing artifacts. Lower-extremity duplex study had not yet been performed at the moment all measured data were captured. Up to this exact time—before the first dose of intravenous antibiotic, any opioid for pain, fluid bolus, or insulin correction—he remained hemodynamically stable with an arterial pressure of 131/79 mm Hg, breathed comfortably through a 0.22 FiO₂ Venturi mask yielding an arterial oxygen tension of 117 mm Hg, exhibited near-normal platelet numbers at 150 500 /µL, maintained a normal serum bilirubin of 0.92 mg/dL, showed mild renal insufficiency with creatinine 1.62 mg/dL and urine output of roughly 1.48 L per day, and retained full neurologic function with a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 in the context of a diabetic foot infection suspected to be transitioning to early sepsis.

args:
A 77-year-old retired army service man with past history of hypercholesterolaemia and previous tuberculosis presented to the Emergency Department having briefly lost consciousness. On recovering, he was aware of a chest discomfort with radiation to the back and neck. He was an ex-smoker. Initial assessment revealed an irregular pulse with a rate between 30 and 50 bpm and a blood pressure of 103/56 mmHg. Cardiac examination was unremarkable with no murmurs heard and the jugular venous pressure was not raised. Auscultation of the chest was also unremarkable. He had different blood pressures between left (103/56 mmHg) and right arms (76/59 mmHg). His ECG revealed atrial fibrillation, a new finding, partial right bundle branch block and left axis deviation but no other ischaemic changes. A CT aortogram excluded aortic dissection and pulmonary embolus. Blood tests revealed an initial troponin T of 96 ng/l (normal < 14), which subsequently rose to 1088 ng/l. He was treated as an acute coronary syndrome. Echocardiography revealed mild left ventricular impairment with lateral wall hypokinesis and no evidence of intra-cardiac thrombus. Whilst on the ward he complained of intermittent chest discomfort and we proceeded to coronary angiography 48 h after admission, which revealed an unobstructed left main stem and minor irregularities within the left anterior descending artery. The right coronary artery was co-dominant and unobstructed. However, the left circumflex (LCx) artery was co-dominant and there was a large area of solid thrombus covering a 2 cm stretch of the artery. Given the history of atrial fibrillation we felt that this most likely represented an embolic occlusion. A guide wire was placed across the occlusion and the thrombus was aspirated successfully with an aspiration catheter. A significant amount of red thrombus was obtained. Subsequent angiographic images of the LCx revealed a completely smooth normal looking artery without any area of stenosis or irregularity. The patient remained well following his procedure and was discharged on apixaban 5 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, bisporolol 2.5 mg daily, clopidogrel 75 mg daily and ramipril 1.25 mg daily.
There was no evidence of an atrial septal defect or suspicion of a patent foramen ovale on the trans-thoracic echocardiogram thus making the diagnosis of an embolic coronary embolus secondary to atrial fibrillation and possibly thrombus in the left atrial appendage the most likely cause of his myocardial infarction.

args:
In March 2015, a 45-year-old Korean woman presented to our emergency department (ED) with shortness of breath and an episode of seizure. Five hours before arriving in the ED, she developed a tonic-clonic seizure for 30 seconds and a high temperature of 40℃. The patient had preceding upper respiratory infection 4 days before coming to the ED. She also complained of severe coughing productive of blood-tinged sputum, and generalized myalgias. Her past medical history was unremarkable except for recurrent (one or two per year) epileptic seizures with oral phenytoin maintenance for 25 years. She had no known allergies. She did not receive the seasonal influenza vaccine.
On physical examination, the patient's blood pressure was 91/59 mmHg, and she had tachycardia (114/min), tachypnea (32/min), and a temperature of 37.9℃. She looked acutely ill, but was mentally alert. Examination of her lungs revealed coarse crackles throughout bilaterally. Other findings were unremarkable. A chest radiograph showed cardiomegaly and bilateral patchy consolidations. An electrocardiogram revealed low QRS voltage in the precordial leads (the entire QRS-complex amplitude was <8 mm). Arterial blood gas analysis revealed pH of 7.38, carbon dioxide tension of 27.7 mmHg, partial pressure of oxygen of 59.8 mmHg, bicarbonate level of 16.7 mmol/L, base excess of 6.4 mmol/L, and oxygen saturation level of 90.7%. The patient was given supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula (2 L/min).
Initial laboratory findings revealed a hemoglobin level of 10.0 g/dL, white blood cell count of 18.8 × 103/mm3 (neutrophils, 85.6%), platelet count of 504 × 103/mm3, blood urea nitrogen level of 14.9 mg/dL, creatinine level of 0.6 mg/dL, and C-reactive protein level of 4.92 mg/L (normal range, 0.0-5.0 mg/L). An influenza rapid antigen test on the patient's nasopharyngeal swab sample, which was promptly performed because of residual seasonal influenza activity in the community, was positive for influenza A virus. She was immediately given a single dose (300 mg) of IV peramivir, and levofloxacin, 500 mg/day.
The patient's dyspnea worsened, however, and she became hypotensive (blood pressure, 86/53 mmHg). She was managed with increased oxygen supply (4 L/min), furosemide, and norepinephrine (0.125 µg/kg/min). Additional blood tests revealed the following component levels: troponin I, 1.36 ng/mL (normal range, 0.0-0.16 ng/mL); creatine kinase-MB (CKMB), 49.27 ng/mL (normal range, 0.0-3.61 ng/mL); creatine phosphokinase (CPK), 1,585 IU/L (normal range, 38-185 IU/L); lactate dehydrogenase, 937 IU/L (normal range, 238-422 IU/L); N-terminal pro b-type natriuretic peptide, 162.8 pg/mL (normal range, 0-125 pg/mL), and myoglobin, 138.8 ng/mL (normal range, 25-58 ng/mL). Serology for hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus and HIV were all negative.
A chest computed tomography scan demonstrated multifocal patch consolidations in both lungs, with ground-glass opacities, suggestive of pulmonary edema or atypical pneumonia. Transthoracic echocardiogram showed a depressed left ventricular (LV) ejection fraction of 30%, LV enlargement, diffuse hypokinesia with sparing LV apex, and decreased right ventricular apical wall motion, which was compatible with acute myocarditis ().
The patient was clinically diagnosed with influenza A-associated fulminant myocarditis, resulting in pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock, and admitted to the intensive care unit. On day 2 of hospitalization, her hemodynamic state had stabilized and she no longer required norepinephrine to maintain blood pressure within a normal range. A follow-up transthoracic echocardiogram taken on day 3 showed improved ventricular systolic function, with LV ejection fraction at 55% to 60%. Laboratory tests on day 4 revealed a troponin I level of 0.168 ng/mL, CK-MB level of 41.31 ng/mL, and CPK level of 1,459 IU/L. On day 5 of her hospitalization, the patient's dyspnea had improved and supplemental oxygen was stopped. On day 6, she was transferred to a general ward. A follow-up chest computed tomography scan demonstrated improved status of pulmonary edema, with residual, mild bilateral pleural effusion. The patient was discharged with recovery on day 9 after admission. Although her muscle enzyme levels still remained elevated 16 days after discharge (CK-MB, 42.63 ng/mL; CPK, 1,340 IU/L), she had been asymptomatic and carefully observed in the outpatient clinic ().
Monitoring of real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction assays (Real-Q Flu A, B Detection Kit; BioSewoom Inc., Seoul, Korea) using the patient's nasopharyngeal aspirates showed positivity for both influenza A (threshold cycle value, 27.23) and subtype hH3N2 (threshold cycle value, 27.39) on day 2 (), but became negative by day 4 of the patient's hospitalization.

args: A 63-year-old man with a fifteen-year history of alcohol-associated cirrhosis—last drink six weeks earlier after enrolling in an outpatient rehabilitation program—walked into the emergency department late this afternoon because over the prior five days his belt no longer reached the third hole, he struggled to bend forward to tie his shoes, and he noted progressive abdominal pressure that forced him to sleep propped on two pillows; he denied confusion, changes in sleep–wake cycle, tremor, personality shifts, or episodes of staring, and he continued to balance his checkbook and complete the daily newspaper crossword without difficulty. Past medical history includes remote chronic hepatitis C successfully treated with ledipasvir/sofosbuvir five years ago, staple line hernia repair following Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease with a baseline creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL. He takes furosemide 40 mg and spironolactone 100 mg each morning, propranolol 20 mg twice daily for portal pressure reduction, and thiamine 100 mg daily; he has no drug allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day, quit intravenous drug use two decades ago, and lives alone in a single-story apartment. Family history is significant for a brother who died of esophageal variceal hemorrhage at 58 and a father with myocardial infarction at 60. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fever, chills, jaundice noted by friends, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, pruritus, shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, palpitations, or recent medication changes. On arrival—before intravenous access, diagnostic paracentesis, diuretics, vitamin K, antibiotics, albumin, or any sedative—vital signs were temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 102/60 mm Hg, heart rate 96 beats/min in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 96 % on ambient air. He was alert, maintained eye contact, recalled three objects after five minutes, spelled the word “world” backward quickly, and held his arms outstretched for twenty seconds without a flapping motion. Sclerae carried a deep yellow tint, conjunctivae were anemic, and the breath emitted a faint odor of stale beer. Dozens of spider angiomata peppered the upper chest and shoulders, palmar erythema was pronounced, and nail beds were pale. Neck veins were flat at 30°. Cardiac examination demonstrated a tachy-but-regular rhythm without murmurs or extra sounds; lungs were clear to auscultation throughout. The abdomen ballooned into a smooth hemisphere with everted umbilicus; percussion revealed tympany centrally that shifted to broad flank dullness when he rolled, and a fluid impulse traversed the abdominal wall without the need for a second examiner’s counter-pressure, though the wall was still soft and not rock-hard. A firm, nodular liver edge was just palpable two centimeters below the right costal margin on deep inspiration, and the spleen was appreciated three fingerbreadths below the left costal margin. There was bilateral 1+ pitting edema to the mid-shins. Neurologic examination showed intact cranial nerves, symmetric 5/5 strength, normal tone, and a narrow-based gait down the corridor. Capillary glucose measured 102 mg/dL. Point-of-care abdominal ultrasonography performed at the bedside prior to phlebotomy demonstrated a shrunken, coarse, nodular liver, patent main portal vein with antegrade flow at 12 cm/s, spleen measuring 18 cm, and a freely mobile anechoic collection layering 6 cm deep in the right paracolic gutter and tracking into the pelvis; no focal hepatic masses or biliary dilation were seen. Laboratory studies drawn at that moment—before any therapeutic intervention—revealed total bilirubin 5.6 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.45 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 54 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 262 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 384 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 97 mmol/L, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/dL, creatinine 1.68 mg/dL, hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 7.6 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 68 × 10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 2.06. Serum ammonia, blood cultures, and diagnostic ascitic fluid studies were sent but results were pending. A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 98 beats/min with a QTc of 444 ms, and an upright chest radiograph revealed elevated hemidiaphragms with basilar atelectasis but no effusion. At this juncture no intravascular fluids, albumin infusions, antibiotics, diuretic adjustments, or procedural interventions had yet been initiated, so the findings above reflect his untreated physiologic status at presentation.

args: A 40-year-old man presented to the community electrophysiology follow-up clinic for his first comprehensive review of chronic anticoagulation that had been started six months earlier, when continuous ambulatory telemetry revealed runs of symptomatic non-valvular atrial fibrillation with ventricular rates up to 150 beats per minute. At discharge he was placed on warfarin with a therapeutic window of 2.0–3.0, and the affiliated pharmacist-run service has scheduled capillary international normalized ratio checks every seven days. The printed log he brings today lists fourteen consecutive readings ranging from 2.1 to 2.6, with a calculated time in therapeutic range exceeding 80 %; he has never required dose interruption, vitamin K rescue, bridging therapy, emergency department evaluation, or hospitalization for over-anticoagulation. His chief concern now is whether the combination of rising home blood-pressure values, heavy drinking, and daily antiplatelet therapy might tip the balance toward a serious hemorrhage. Hypertension was first documented three years ago during a pre-employment examination with a seated manual pressure of 164/92 mm Hg; he was placed on lisinopril 20 mg each morning, yet recent home cuff recordings average 152–158 mm Hg systolic. In clinic today, after five minutes of rest in a seated position, the manual measurement is 156/94 mm Hg with an irregularly irregular pulse of 82 beats per minute, respirations 14 per minute, oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air, temperature 36.8 °C, and body mass index 27 kg/m². Six months before his arrhythmia diagnosis he underwent percutaneous coronary intervention with a drug-eluting stent to the proximal left anterior descending artery for an anterior non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction and has since taken chewable aspirin 81 mg every morning for secondary prevention; cardiology initially prescribed clopidogrel for three months, which has been discontinued. For chronic lumbar discogenic pain exacerbated by his job as a warehouse supervisor, he self-treats with over-the-counter naproxen sodium 220 mg once or twice weekly, most recently two days ago after lifting heavy boxes. He also takes sertraline 50 mg nightly for generalized anxiety disorder initiated by his primary clinician two years earlier. He has never experienced an ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, transient ischemic attack, intracranial aneurysm, gastrointestinal bleeding, genitourinary bleeding, hemarthrosis, epistaxis requiring medical care, or need for transfusion; he reports no personal or family history of hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, platelet dysfunction, or connective tissue disorders. Review of systems is negative for melena, hematochezia, hematemesis, hematuria, easy bruising, gingival oozing, jaundice, pruritus, right-upper-quadrant pain, encephalopathy, abdominal distention, peripheral edema, visual disturbance, focal weakness, or new headaches. Social history is notable for substantial alcohol intake: two 12-ounce bottles of craft beer with lunch and dinner on weekdays and five to six mixed drinks while socializing on each weekend day, totaling approximately 31 alcoholic beverages per week; he denies morning drinking, blackouts, withdrawal tremor, pancreatitis, or prior counseling to reduce consumption. He has never smoked cigarettes and denies illicit drug use. Physical examination reveals an alert, well-nourished man in no acute distress with normal mentation, anicteric sclerae, moist oral mucosa without petechiae, a supple neck without jugular venous distention, clear lung fields, and a variable first heart sound without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender with no palpable hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, spider angiomata, or abdominal wall varices; extremities are warm with no edema, ecchymoses, or purpura, and distal pulses are 2+ and symmetric. Neurologic screening demonstrates intact cranial nerves, full strength, normal coordination, and a steady gait. Point-of-care capillary hemoglobin is 15.0 g/dL, platelet count from last week was 238 × 10⁹/L, and today’s finger-stick INR prior to dosing reads 2.3. A comprehensive metabolic panel drawn three days ago shows serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 108 mL/min/1.73 m², blood urea nitrogen 13 mg/dL, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 28 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 26 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, and albumin 4.4 g/dL, indicating preserved hepatic and renal function. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained in clinic confirms atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response at 78 beats per minute and no acute ST-T changes. args: A 62-year-old woman with a history of insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by distal symmetric neuropathy, permanent atrial fibrillation on apixaban, stage-3 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL), and remote coronary artery bypass grafting was brought to the emergency department by municipal EMS after being discovered at 06:45 lying on the kitchen floor by her spouse, who had heard a thud from an adjacent room; she was reportedly last seen well at 05:30 preparing coffee and had not complained of headache, chest pain, or dyspnea earlier that morning. Paramedics arrived eight minutes after the 911 call, found her breathing on her own with a patent airway, seated her upright against a wall, applied a rigid cervical collar as a precaution against an unwitnessed fall, and transferred her onto a padded stretcher without administering dextrose, benzodiazepines, opioids, antiemetics, paralytics, hyperosmolar agents, or intravenous fluids other than a 5 mL saline flush through a single 18-gauge catheter placed in the left antecubital fossa. Finger-stick glucose in the field was 138 mg/dL and an on-board cardiac monitor demonstrated irregularly irregular rhythm at 96–104 beats/min with no acute ST-segment changes. Oxygen at 4 L/min by nasal cannula was provided solely to maintain saturation above 96 %. The 12-minute transport was uneventful, and no additional medications were given so that the hospital team could evaluate her neurologic state unaltered. She rolled into our resuscitation bay at 07:14, clothing damp from spilled coffee but free of blood or vomitus, still verbally responsive though intermittently incoherent. Simultaneous with the initial in-house survey—performed before any drug, imaging contrast, or fluid bolus—vital signs were blood pressure 148/78 mm Hg in the right arm, heart rate 102 beats/min in atrial fibrillation, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min with adequate tidal excursion, oxygen saturation 97 % on the prehospital nasal cannula, and core temperature 36.6 °C measured rectally. Airway assessment revealed moist mucosa, intact dentition, midline uvula, and a brisk gag when a Yankauer catheter touched the posterior pharynx; cough remained strong and protective. Both pupils were 3 mm, round, and brisk to direct and consensual light. During a 30-second period of quiet observation her eyelids stayed closed without flutter; however, the moment her surname was spoken in a normal tone just above either ear, the lids lifted to approximately three-quarters aperture, her gaze tracked toward the speaker, and remained open for several seconds before drifting shut again when questioning ceased. Speech was fluent yet disordered: she correctly stated her first name but insisted the location was her kitchen and the year was “2029,” repeatedly asking if the staff wanted breakfast. She misidentified the examining clinician as her late brother, laughed inappropriately, and provided rambling answers to orientation questions, though articulation and grammar were intact. Cranial nerve testing showed symmetric facial movement, palate elevation in the midline, and tongue protrusion without deviation; extra-ocular movements were full and smooth. Baseline limb tone was mildly increased in the lower extremities, normal in the upper. When asked, she squeezed the examiner’s fingers bilaterally with equal strength, lifted both arms to shoulder level for five seconds, wiggled toes on command, and rolled her eyes upward upon request, all without hesitation. Motor strength was 5/5 throughout by Medical Research Council grading, and there was no pronator drift. Deep tendon reflexes were 2 + and symmetric at biceps, triceps, patellae, and Achilles; plantar stimulation yielded flexor responses bilaterally. Sensation to light touch was grossly intact in all dermatomes, though she reported “pins and needles” in both feet consistent with known neuropathy. An attempt at rapid finger-to-nose testing revealed slow yet accurate targeting; heel-to-shin was steady. No abnormal flexor or extensor posturing, myoclonus, or tremor was observed during the examination. Cardiovascular auscultation documented an irregularly irregular S1 and S2 without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; distal pulses were 2 + with capillary refill under two seconds and warm extremities. Lungs were clear to auscultation with resonant percussion and equal breath sounds bilaterally. Abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender; bowel sounds were present. Pelvic stability was preserved to gentle compression, and rectal tone was intact on digital exam. Inspection of the scalp disclosed a 1.5 cm superficial abrasion over the left parietal region with minimal dried blood but no palpable step-off or crepitus. No long-bone deformities, rib tenderness, or spinal tenderness were noted. A focused bedside ultrasound revealed no pericardial effusion and no free intra-abdominal fluid. args: An 81-year-old Asian man presented to our department complaining of fever since the preceding day. The patient had been under treatment for the previous 3 years for chronic heart failure and chronic renal failure. He did not have a history of malignancy, diabetes mellitus, cytotoxic therapy, or corticosteroid use, and no foreign bodies had been implanted. The patient’s family history was unremarkable. Physical examination revealed a heart rate of 101 beats/min, blood pressure of 87/48 mmHg, respiratory rate of 20 breaths/min, temperature of 37.0 °C, and oxygen saturation of 87% on room air. He had no caries or periodontitis. Results of respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Limb examination demonstrated mild edema of both legs. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) showed a low-density mass in the right iliopsoas muscle indicative of an iliopsoas abscess (Fig. ). The patient’s white blood cell count, C-reactive protein (CRP), and procalcitonin levels were 19,400/μl, 13.35 mg/dl, and 3.950 ng/ml, respectively. Serum blood urea nitrogen and creatinine were elevated at 77.2 mg/dl and 3.69 mg/dl, respectively. A CT-guided percutaneous drainage of the psoas abscess was performed, and an indwelling catheter was placed. Gram staining of the drained fluid revealed many neutrophils and Gram-positive streptococci. On the basis of these findings, a presumptive diagnosis of iliopsoas abscess caused by Streptococcus species was made, and treatment with ampicillin/sulbactam (ABPC/SBT) 1.5 g, administered intravenously every 8 h, was initiated. Results of organism cultures of the abscess and blood were positive, and P. micra was identified by using the API ZYM system (Sysmex-bioMérieux Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), with the organism exhibiting susceptibility to penicillin G, ampicillin, clindamycin, and meropenem. By day 7, the patient’s white blood cell count normalized. By day 20, his CRP level was decreased to 0.35 mg/dl. Therefore, the pigtail catheter was removed. The patient died of peritonitis due to colon diverticulum perforation after 5 weeks of treatment. An autopsy revealed no right iliopsoas abscess at the time of death.

args: A 55-year-old man with a decade-long history of compensated cirrhosis attributed to chronic hepatitis B infection and intermittent heavy beer consumption was escorted to the emergency department by his spouse after she noticed that over the preceding two days he had grown unusually forgetful—misplacing his car keys, repeating the same question about the time of an upcoming clinic visit, and once calling their adult son by his brother’s name—yet remained able to ambulate independently, feed himself, and dress without assistance. Earlier that morning he had driven to a nearby grocery store but returned home after realizing in the parking lot that he could not recall what he intended to purchase; on the drive back he scraped the passenger-side mirror against the mailbox, an error he typically would have avoided. He denied visual hallucinations, seizures, syncope, headache, fever, chills, chest pain, dyspnea, gastrointestinal bleeding, melena, hematemesis, new abdominal distention, or limb swelling. His regular medications included entecavir 0.5 mg daily, a multivitamin, and over-the-counter lactulose 20 g twice nightly that he admitted missing intermittently because of bothersome bloating; he took no diuretics, proton-pump inhibitors, or opioids, and had never undergone variceal band ligation or transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement. He quit smoking fifteen years earlier after a 20-pack-year history and drank approximately six cans of beer each weekend until three months ago, when his hepatologist advised strict abstinence. There was no family history of liver disease, and he worked as an accountant. On arrival, before any pharmacologic or intravenous intervention, vital signs were: temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 118/64 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 96 % on room air. He appeared alert but required a few extra seconds to answer orientation questions, initially placing the month one month early before quickly correcting himself; his speech was coherent though somewhat slowed, and he completed a reverse-serial-sevens task with two errors. When asked to extend his arms and dorsiflex his wrists, a brief irregular flapping motion became evident after several seconds. Pupils were equal and reactive, extra-ocular movements full, and there was no focal motor or sensory deficit. Skin inspection revealed faint jaundice of the sclerae, scattered spider angiomata over the upper chest, and mild palmar erythema; there were no bruises or petechiae. The neck was supple without jugular venous distention. Cardiac examination uncovered a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and lungs were clear to auscultation. His abdomen was scaphoid with normal respiratory excursion of the abdominal wall; percussion produced a resonant note throughout, flank contours were flat, and shifting dullness could not be demonstrated even with the patient rolled onto either side. Light palpation elicited no tenderness, the liver edge was firm and mildly nodular two centimeters below the right costal margin on deep inspiration, and the spleen tip was not palpable. No abdominal wall collateral veins were visible, and the umbilicus remained slightly concave. There was no peripheral edema. Rectal examination showed brown stool that tested guaiac-negative. Capillary glucose measured 102 mg/dL. Laboratory studies drawn immediately on presentation demonstrated: total bilirubin 2.7 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.85 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 52 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 76 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 188 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 205 U/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 99 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 22 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.9 × 10³/µL, platelet count 102 × 10³/µL, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 2.35. Serum ammonia level was pending at the time of initial assessment. Hepatitis B viral DNA obtained two months earlier had been 1,200 IU/mL, and alpha-fetoprotein at that time was 6 ng/mL. Urinalysis performed on arrival showed moderate bilirubin without leukocyte esterase or nitrites. Point-of-care abdominal ultrasonography confirmed a shrunken, coarsely nodular liver with patent portal and hepatic veins exhibiting hepatopetal flow at 15 cm/s; importantly, no free fluid was detected in Morrison’s pouch, the paracolic gutters, or pelvis, and the spleen measured 16 cm in maximal length. A portable chest radiograph revealed elevated hemidiaphragms but clear lung fields, and a 12-lead electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm at 86 beats/min with a QTc of 442 ms and no ischemic changes. Blood cultures and a diagnostic work-up for precipitating factors such as infection, gastrointestinal bleeding, or electrolyte disturbance were initiated while he remained hemodynamically stable and cooperative, albeit mildly slowed in cognition.

A 59-year-old woman with a history of recent placement of a dual-chamber permanent pacemaker (PPM) due to sick sinus syndrome was admitted to the hospital due to erythema, warmth, and fluctuance over the PPM site (). She was at 4 weeks after PPM placement. Her symptoms developed gradually over a course of 5 days. She denied any fever or chills. She did not have any specific exposures over the PPM area other than dressing changes and cleaning with water and soap. There was no recent travel or pet exposure. Her vital signs on admission showed a temperature of 36.5°C, heart rate of 93 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, and blood pressure of 100/60 mmHg. Physical examination on admission revealed a nondistressed woman, and her chest showed erythema overlying the PPM site with pain to palpation, fluctuance, and evidence of pus from surgical scar (). Her laboratory workup showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 7300/μL, with 80.3% neutrophils, hemoglobin (Hgb) of 12.8 g/dL, platelet count of 189000/μL, lactate of 1.4 mmol/L, glucose of 142 mg/dL, BUN of 9 mg/dL, and creatinine of 0.66 mg/dL.
On the day of admission, blood cultures were obtained, and she was started on broad-spectrum antibiotics with vancomycin 1250 mg IV every 12 hours and piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 hours. She then underwent PPM removal on the 2nd day of hospitalization. Dual-chamber leads were taken out, and intraoperative specimens from the pocket site were sent to microbiology for stains and cultures. Pathology samples were not sent except for the leads for gross pathological evaluation. No frank pus was obtained intraoperatively, and the leads were removed without any complications. Blood cultures remained negative, and initial gram stain from the intraoperative specimens showed no organisms and rare WBCs. On day 4 of hospitalization, microbiology reported scant growth of acid-fast bacilli from the deep wound cultures, and empiric therapy was changed to amikacin 10 mg/kg IV daily, imipenem 1 g IV twice daily, and linezolid 600 mg PO twice daily. The specimen was then sent to the National Jewish Health reference laboratory (Denver, Colorado, USA) for identification by rpoB gene sequencing, which showed Mycobacterium peregrinum.
The patient showed clinical improvement and was discharged from the hospital on day 5 with the proposed empiric intravenous regimen. At 3 weeks of follow-up, the patient reported no side effects from the antibiotics prescribed and had proper healing of the PPM pocket site. Full susceptibilities by broth microtiter dilution methodology were made available (), and she was switched to moxifloxacin 400 mg PO daily and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily to complete a 4-month regimen. Of note, the erm(39) gene was not tested for this case, and it was not preincubated in subinhibitory concentrations of macrolide to check for inducible resistance.
The patient was seen at 2 months and completed therapy at 4 months with proper healing of the PPM pocket site (). She had no side effects from the antibiotics and did not require reimplantation of the PPM.
