args: A 46-year-old woman is evaluated this morning in the surgical step-down unit before transfer to the operating theatre for a planned three-port laparoscopic removal of a perforated gangrenous appendix that was identified on overnight computed tomography; the general-surgery team anticipates approximately 60–90 minutes of pneumoperitoneum with possible washout and placement of a drain. She has been an inpatient for the past nine days, spending almost the entirety of that time supine with the head of the bed elevated to 30 degrees because standing or even dangling her legs precipitates sharp right-lower-quadrant pain and light-headedness. Nursing flow sheets document fewer than 250 cumulative steps this week, and physical therapy sessions were deferred until the infectious process was controlled. Her current admission followed an outside-hospital emergency-department visit eleven days ago for fevers to 39.5 °C, rigors, tachycardia in the 130s, and systolic pressures in the low 90s; blood cultures at that facility subsequently grew Escherichia coli in both aerobic and anaerobic bottles, serum lactate peaked at 4.2 mmol L⁻¹, and she met systemic inflammatory response criteria for septic shock. She required aggressive fluid resuscitation and a 48-hour norepinephrine infusion in the intensive-care unit prior to transfer to the ward, and broad-spectrum antibiotics were continued once sensitivities returned. Because peripheral access repeatedly failed during resuscitation, a right internal-jugular triple-lumen catheter was placed under ultrasound guidance on hospital day one; the dressing remains pristine this morning, there is no erythema or tenderness along the tract, and all ports draw and flush easily. No other indwelling vascular devices, tunneled lines, or ports have ever been placed. She has not undergone any operative or endoscopic procedure within the preceding month, and the index infection represents her only hospitalization in more than a decade. Past medical history is notable only for chronic varicosities over both calves that she first noticed during her third pregnancy; on inspection today the great saphenous distribution on each leg displays dilated, serpiginous veins, but skin temperature is symmetric, calf circumferences are equal, and there is no pitting edema or tenderness to palpation. She has never experienced crampy calf pain, unexplained dyspnea, hemoptysis, or imaging-confirmed venous or pulmonary clot, and no first-degree relative has carried a diagnosis of thrombosis or thrombo­philia. She denies prior myocardial ischemia, episodes of pump failure, chronic lung disease, inflammatory bowel disorders, connective-tissue disease, or malignant neoplasms, and she has not suffered pneumonia, cerebrovascular accident, spinal cord injury, hip/pelvic/leg fracture, multiple trauma, or placement of an immobilizing plaster cast at any point during this admission or within the past month. She has also never developed thrombocytopenia attributable to heparin exposure. The patient is gravida 3 para 3, has always been normotensive outside pregnancy, and has not used estrogen-containing contraceptives in more than ten years. Medications at present include piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every six hours, acetaminophen as needed for fever, and a daily proton-pump inhibitor; she is not receiving anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, erythropoiesis-stimulating drugs, corticosteroids, or hormone therapy. She quit smoking six years ago after a 12-pack-year history, drinks wine socially once or twice per month, and does not use recreational substances. Overnight vital signs: temperature 37.4 °C, heart rate 96 beats min⁻¹ in sinus rhythm, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16 min⁻¹, oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air; body-mass index calculated at 25 kg m⁻². She appears tired but converses clearly, is oriented to person, place, and time, and reports that the constant abdominal ache has eased slightly since the last dose of hydromorphone six hours ago. Sclerae are anicteric, oral airway is Mallampati class II with intact dentition, neck veins are not distended, and carotid upstrokes are brisk. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm with normal S₁ and S₂, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Lung fields are clear to auscultation with good expansion. Abdomen shows a soft but distended contour, localized guarding over McBurney’s point, and mild rebound; bowel sounds are diminished. Lower extremities demonstrate warm skin, palpable posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses 2+ bilaterally, absence of cords, equal calf circumferences of 36 cm, and the previously mentioned superficial venous prominences. Neurologic screening identifies full strength in all extremities that are not limited by pain, intact sensation to light touch, and symmetric reflexes. A 60-year-old male came to the emergency department of a tertiary care hospital, in Pakistan, with complaints of fever for one week, cough, and shortness of breath for two days. He was tested positive for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) before admission. The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test came positive and chest X-ray revealed bilateral infiltrates as shown in Figure . The patient was diagnosed with severe COVID-19 infection and was admitted to our dedicated COVID-19 ICU. His past medical history included essential hypertension for the past 10 years, which was in control by his medication.
Examination at the time of admissions showed a regular heart rate of 100 beats per minute, non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP) of 155/88 mmHg, temperature 37.5°C, respiratory rate of 30 breaths per minute, and pulse oximeter showed 80% saturation on room air. The patient was immediately given non-invasive 100% FiO2, which improved saturation to 95%. Continuous monitoring of vitals was done using the Life Scope VS BSM-3000 series monitor (Nihon Kohden, Tokyo, Japan) for NIBP measurement, pulse oximetry, and electrocardiogram.
Initial lab investigations showed an elevated white blood cell (WBC) count of 13.9×109/L, elevated neutrophil percentage of 91%, decreased lymphocyte percentage of 6%, and a decreased platelet count of 95×109/L. The biochemical examination displayed normal serum creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, slightly elevated blood urea nitrogen 25 mg/dL, serum lactate dehydrogenase 1397 U/L. C-reactive protein (CRP) was markedly increased with a level of 82.5 mg/L, and procalcitonin was normal at 0.3 ng/mL. Coagulation function displayed an elevated D-dimer level of 1.96 mg/L, normal prothrombin time of 14.2 seconds, elevated international normalized ratio of 1.35, and elevated activated partial thromboplastin time of 28.4 seconds on Day 1. Arterial blood gases showed a slightly alkalotic pH of 7.48, decreased pCO2 of 27 mmHg, decreased pO2 of 60.7 mmHg, and increased lactate level of 3.2 mmol/L. His laboratory findings are shown in Table . However, one of the limitations to this study is that his complete cytokine assay was not performed.
The patient's treatment plan included antibiotics, prophylactic enoxaparin, 40 mg subcutaneous once daily, symptomatic, and supportive treatment, including steroids and vitamins. The patient was also found to have CRS by aggravated inflammatory markers, for example, CRP levels []. Intravenous TCZ 6 mg per kg was given on the day of admission. Oral feed along with oral and intravenous hydration was continued.
On the third day of admission, the patient's oxygen requirements decreased. The WBC count increased to 45×109/L, pO2 increased to 82 mmHg, and inflammatory markers were reduced. Chest X-ray also showed improvement. On the same day, the patient began complaining of pain in the right lower limb. A physical examination revealed hypothermia, numbness, and mild blackening of the right foot as shown in Figures , . Peripheral pulses of the right foot were not palpable. The vascular ultrasound of lower limbs showed no vascular flow in the popliteal, anterior tibial, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis artery. A severe arterial insufficiency was diagnosed, and the Vascular Surgery department was consulted immediately. They declared the limb unsalvageable and counselled for right limb above-knee amputation after the patient's condition had stabilized. Administering a therapeutic anticoagulant dose to prevent any other athero-embolic event was also suggested. Enoxaparin was then increased to 80 mg subcutaneous twice daily. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were added for the raised white cell count. On the ninth day of admission, the patient was maintaining oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Chest X-ray showed a significant reduction in bilateral patchy infiltrates as shown in Figure . The right foot was blackened and atrophied, which extended up to the knee. No progression in ischemia was noted. The patient was stepped down to high dependency unit (HDU) and was placed on the vascular surgery operating room list for above-knee amputation. The surgery took place under spinal anesthesia. After all aseptic measures, fish-mouth incision was given at the mid femur level through the skin, superficial fascia, and subcutaneous tissue. Deep tissue dissection and muscle transection was done. Neurovascular structures were identified, ligated, and transected. Lastly, the bone was lacerated, hemostasis was secured, and the wound was closed. Aseptic pressure dressing was applied, and the patient remained hemodynamically stable. Postoperatively, the patient was monitored in the HDU for 48 hours. The patient was then discharged and advised tablet rivaroxaban 10 mg once daily for one month.

A 43-year-old male patient presented to the emergency department with a 20-day history of fever, predominantly at nights and headaches. His previous medical history is unremarkable; he only referred to a 20-year history of smoking and denied use of intravenous or recreational drugs, any prior sexually transmitted diseases, or blood transfusions. At presentation, the patient had a blood pressure of 109/75 mmHg, heart rate 87 bpm, respiration rate 24 breaths/minute, temperature 38.5°C, and oxygen saturation of 70% at atmospheric pressure. Physical examination revealed pale skin and mucosa; tongue and soft palate had lesions consistent with oral candidiasis and congestive pharynx. Pulmonary fields revealed decreased sounds without crackles or wheezing and painful hepatomegaly and extremities with hyperchromic nodular lesions on both ankles, suggestive of Kaposi sarcoma. Due to these findings, initial blood tests included HIV serology that came out positive; CBC: Hb 11.1 g/dL, hematocrit 33.8%, WBC 7,100/mm3, lymphocytes 3%, and neutrophils 95%; IgE 788.2 UI/ml; VSG 77 mm/hr; TGO 91 UI/L; TGP 66 UI/L; and DHL 2250 UI/L. Chest X-ray revealed disseminated infiltrates in both lungs. Medical management was initiated with omeprazole, metamizole, and oxygen with nasal prongs 3 L/min. On day 2 of hospitalization, the bronchoscopy fluid examination resulted positive for C. albicans and negative for other pathogenic bacteria and fungi. However, we decided to start dexamethasone 6 mg IV, trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg, nebulization with ipratropium, and budesonide due to high suspicion of P. jirovecii infection. On day 3 of hospitalization, HIV infection was confirmed with a viral load of 531,000 copies/ml and CD4+ T-cell count of 11 cells/mm3. Other studies were performed including a PPD (negative test 0 mm) and anticytomegalovirus serology (IgG positive). On day 4 of hospitalization, the infectious disease division started ART with ritonavir/lopinavir, tenofovir, and emtricitabine. During the subsequent days, the patient showed clinical improvement. However, on day 13, his clinical condition declined with progressive dyspnea, severe dysphagia, and abdominal pain. Auscultation showed basal rales in both lungs; a new chest X-ray demonstrated no changes compared with the previous one. New blood tests included CBC: Hb 14.5 g/dl, hematocrit 44%, VCM 90 fL, platelets 284,000 × 103, WBC 18,400/mm3, neutrophils 89%, and lymphocytes 5%. Serum electrolytes, glucose, BUN, and creatinine had no alterations. He developed dysphagia and episodes of oxygen desaturation partially corrected with nasal prongs. On day 19 of hospitalization, due to continuous episodes of low oxygen saturation, a chest CT-scan was ordered (), showing generalized lung involvement and mediastinal adenopathy (Video 1 in Supplementary Material ).
Supplementary Video 1 showed digitalized CT-scan of the thorax in coronal sectioning.
Later that day, an endoscopy showed chronic reflux esophagitis (Grade D of the Los Angeles Classification System), with ulceration (Figures and ). On day 23 of hospitalization, dyspnea worsened; oxygen supply was administered with continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP). A pulmonary biopsy was performed on day 24, which reported CMV pneumonia (Figures and ); the patient was transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to persistent hypoxemia and was started on methylprednisolone 500 mg IV (maintained for 2 days) and ganciclovir 500 mg IV, and ART was changed to efavirenz, emtricitabine, and tenofovir, to decrease pill intake (1 pill/day). Intravenous sedation was initiated and oxygen supply was maintained with CPAP. The patient had 2 episodes of heart failure that required management with furosemide and nitroglycerin. He developed fever and respiratory distress with episodes of delirium treated with antipsychotics; we suspected the development of IRIS due to a paradoxical worsening of his condition despite being treated with ART and ganciclovir. Therefore, we started methylprednisolone and thalidomide 100 mg/day for immunosuppression and immunomodulation, respectively. After 17 days in the ICU, the oxygen requirements began to drop and the mental status improved. He remained hemodynamically stable with clinical and radiological improvemt. args: An 86-year-old man who lives alone in a one-story bungalow and still insists on mowing his own lawn was brought to the medical intensive care intake area by advanced life-support ambulance after a neighbor noticed him sitting on his porch looking pale and slightly breathless late this afternoon; according to the neighbor he had complained earlier of a “touch of the flu,” took two acetaminophen tablets, and lay down but received no prescription medications, inhalers, intravenous fluids, or supplemental oxygen until the paramedics arrived. His past history, obtained from a well-kept wallet card and verified by electronic chart review, lists treated hypertension and cataract extraction only; he has never required organ transplantation, dialysis, chemotherapy, chronic corticosteroids, or biologic agents and has no record of cirrhosis, chronic lung disease, advanced heart failure, or immunodeficiency. Paramedics found him seated upright, conversant though mildly confused about the exact date, with an initial automated blood pressure of 142/82 mm Hg that was reproduced on a second cuff; pulse oximetry on ambient air showed 90 %, so they placed a simple face mask attached to the truck blender delivering 38 % inspired oxygen, started one peripheral line without running fluids, and gave no bronchodilators, diuretics, antibiotics, analgesics, or vasopressors during the eight-minute transport. On arrival to the resuscitation bay—and before any emergency department intervention—the first complete set of vitals was captured: oral temperature 98.5 °F, non-invasive blood pressure 140/80 mm Hg confirmed with the manual sphygmomanometer, regular pulse 95 beats per minute displayed on telemetry, and a visually counted respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute without accessory-muscle use. The patient opened his eyes to voice, answered most orientation questions but stumbled on the month, and obeyed two-step commands, yielding a Glasgow Coma Score of 14 in the absence of ethanol or sedatives. Skin was warm and dry with brisk capillary refill; no mottling or peripheral cyanosis was present. Neck veins were not distended at 30°, cardiac auscultation revealed a crisp S1, single physiologic S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and distal pulses were 2+ and symmetric. Breath sounds were vesicular throughout with a few scattered end-inspiratory crackles at both lung bases; percussion remained resonant and there was no wheeze. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender with active bowel sounds, and there was no flank costovertebral angle pain. Extremities showed no edema, clubbing, or tenderness, and a brief neurologic screen demonstrated equal pupils, symmetric strength, and intact coordination when he touched his nose. A right radial arterial specimen drawn nine minutes after stretcher placement—while the face mask continued to deliver the same 38 % oxygen—yielded a pH of 7.43, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 79 mm Hg; end-tidal CO₂ on waveform capnography was 34 mm Hg. Point-of-care basic metabolic panel returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.5 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 21 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, the last value matching his outpatient baseline recorded at a primary-care visit two months earlier. A complete blood count drawn from the same stick showed white blood cells 12 800 /mm³ with 80 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 34 %, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, and platelets 206 000 /µL. Venous lactate measured 1.9 mmol/L. A portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained before any hospital therapy revealed mild bilateral bibasilar infiltrates and elevated hemidiaphragms without pleural effusion, cardiomegaly, or pneumothorax. Twelve-lead electrocardiography performed during the arterial draw demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 96 beats per minute, PR interval 180 ms, QRS 94 ms, and nonspecific lateral T-wave flattening but no acute ST-segment elevation or depression. Bedside focused cardiac ultrasound showed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle with an inferior vena cava diameter of 1.4 cm that collapsed more than 50 % on inspiration, suggesting euvolemia rather than congestion. Bladder scan revealed 120 mL of urine, and he reported having voided shortly before the ambulance arrived. Importantly, during the thirty minutes between EMS arrival and acceptance by the critical-care team, he received no intravenous crystalloids, antibiotics, bronchodilators, diuretics, vasopressors, corticosteroids, or transfusions; thus the physiologic and laboratory data—temperature 98.5 °F, systolic pressure 140 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 80 mm Hg, heart rate 95 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.43, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.5 mmol/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, hematocrit 34 %, white blood cell count 12 800 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 38 %, and arterial oxygen tension 79 mm Hg—represent his untreated presentation for what is presumed to be early community-acquired pneumonia complicated by mild hypoxemia and transient confusion.

A 58-year-old male with a history of well-controlled hypertension, chronic smoking, and recently diagnosed biopsy-confirmed small cell lung cancer (SCLC) of the left upper lobe presented to the hospital with a left field cut, right gaze palsy, and left hand weakness (). He had previously been admitted to an outside hospital after developing sudden-onset tonic-clonic seizures and expressive aphasia. A magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan of the brain at that time revealed scattered subcentimeter ischemic infarcts in the right and left frontal lobes, right parietal lobe, and the left cerebellar hemisphere. He was transferred to our institution for further management.
Upon admission, the patient was admitted to the neurosciences intensive care unit for further monitoring. He was placed on continuous electroencephalographic monitoring. Computed tomography (CT) of the brain showed new mild hypodensities in the right subcortical frontal lobe with stable hypodensities in the periventricular white matter, left subcortical frontal and left occipital lobes (). A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) revealed a moderately dilated left atrium with a 40 × 39 mm spherical echogenic, heterogeneous mass originating from a left pulmonary vein (, ). Given that the patient had no significant cardiovascular history, it was concluded that the embolic infarcts were likely secondary to tumor shower from the left atrial metastasis from his primary SCLC. The patient was scheduled to undergo cardiac MRI imaging to further characterize the left atrial lesion and extent of invasion, but given the risk of intubation and sedation, this was deferred. A CT-angiogram and chest X-ray of the chest was conducted which demonstrated encasement of the left main pulmonary artery with direct extension of the left pulmonary vein and left atrium (, ).
In the setting of metastatic left atrial invasion with metastatic thromboembolic spread to the brain, the patient was started on anticoagulation with a heparin drip in the hospital and later transitioned to enoxaparin. His neurologic status improved and he was transferred to the oncology service for initiation of chemotherapy. After initiation, he developed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response that was subsequently controlled with metoprolol. He was discharged in stable condition from the hospital and has been following up with his oncologist and cardio-oncologist for continued management of SCLC.

args:
A 45-year-old male patient arrived at our otorhinolaryngology department for an emergency examination. Owing to the policy of his employing company, he was obliged to self-test for SARS-CoV-2 twice a week. Four hours before his arrival, the patient had attempted to perform a SARS-CoV-2 rapid antigen test (GenBody Inc, Cheonan, KR) at home by taking an OPS. Upon specimen collection, he felt a mild resistance and had a gag reflex, which resulted in the ingestion of the swab applicator.
The patient showed no signs of respiratory distress and had stable vital signs. He showed no apparent regurgitation of saliva. We performed a SARS-CoV-2 rapid antigen test (Hangzhou Clungene Biotech, CN) by taking an NPS and obtained a negative result.
During routine ear, nose, and throat examination, including flexible rhinopharyngolaryngoscopy, we found no foreign body in the upper respiratory tract or at the pharyngeal level.
We performed thoracic and abdominal computed tomography (CT) scans in the supine and right lateral recumbent positions. Swab applicators are usually made of nylon. Thus, their radiopacity is not guaranteed without a documented radiopaque marking. In our case, we did not have the manufacturer’s description in hand. To overcome this problem, we asked the patient to drink water to fill the stomach with fluid. After proper windowing, a radiolucent band was visible in the stomach, suggestive of a foreign body (Figure ). No other abnormalities were observable in the imaged regions.
In collaboration with the department of gastroenterology, we performed an urgent gastroscopy with a flexible endoscope (Olympus, JP). Despite four hours having elapsed since the incident, we still identified the swab stick intact in the stomach. After firmly catching the object with a polypectomy loop, it was withdrawn and removed safely (Figure ). A second look showed no signs of mucosal damage or any residual foreign bodies along the esophagus and stomach. We examined the foreign body and confirmed that it had been removed without missing parts (Figure ). After eventless observation, the patient was discharged on the same day.
Informed consent was obtained from the patient for all the procedures and for the publication of information on his clinical course. All actions were made in accordance with the World Medical Association’s Declaration of Helsinki.

args:
Fourteen months prior to the referral to our institution, a 69-year-old Japanese woman presented at an orthopedic hospital with a painless mass, approximately 10 mm in diameter, in the right breast adjacent to the sternal border. Based on contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) findings, the breast mass was tentatively diagnosed as a benign tumor. However, the lesion gradually increased in size. Four months later, she was referred to the breast surgery department of a community hospital. Ultrasound imaging showed a 17 mm mass surrounded by fluid in the right breast. Cytology of the needle biopsy specimens revealed nonspecific inflammatory cells without atypical cells. Bacterial cultures of the specimens yielded no pathogens. She was administered cefcapene pivoxil 100 mg three times a day for 12 days; however, there was no improvement. She was diagnosed with nonspecific (non-purulent) mastitis, and incisional drainage of the lesion was performed.
Eight months later, the breast lesion, which still continued to increase in size, spontaneously discharged pus from the skin around the right nipple. The patient was referred to the breast surgery department at our hospital, and further referred to our department for the treatment of intractable mastitis.
Her medical history revealed miliary tuberculosis at the age of 25 years, for which she had been hospitalized and treated with three drugs, including streptomycin, for 1 year. She had been a homemaker since her twenties, living with her husband. Her socioeconomic status was not low. She had five pregnancies, three abortions, and two live births. She was an ex-smoker with a smoking history of 42 pack-years. Currently, she was taking clopidogrel and aspirin for ischemic heart disease, bisoprolol and enalapril for hypertension, and lovastatin for dyslipidemia. Her family history was unremarkable.
On examination, she appeared well with stable vital signs: body temperature, 36.7°C; blood pressure, 144/76 mm Hg; pulse rate, 96 beats per minute; and respiratory rate, 14 breaths per minute. The cervical and axillary lymph nodes were not palpable. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs, and the lung sounds were clear without crackles. Her abdomen was soft and flat with no hepatosplenomegaly. Clinical breast examination revealed palpable cord-like induration with mild tenderness from the right margin of the sternum to the skin around the right nipple. The formation of a fistula was observed near the right nipple, and it showed slightly cloudy and yellowish exudates (Fig. a).
The laboratory test results were as follows: white blood cell count, 5200/µL; hemoglobin, 11.9 g/dL; platelet count, 159,000/µL; albumin, 4.5 g/dL; aspartate aminotransferase, 21 U/L; creatinine, 0.65 mg/dL; and C-reactive protein, 0.08 mg/dL (reference: < 0.06 mg/dL). Interferon-gamma release assay (T-SPOT.TB®) showed positive results. She tested negative for human immunodeficiency virus.
Mammography showed distortion accompanied by retraction of the right nipple with regional microcalcifications. CT revealed high-density lesions suggesting inflammatory changes in the right breast and calcifications of the right apical pleura and mediastinal lymph nodes. Breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed no mass in the right mammary gland and a fistula formation between the parasternal area and skin of the right breast (Fig. a). T2 imaging showed enhanced high signal intensity lesions in the sternum, suggesting sternal osteomyelitis.
Pus drained from the fistula was collected with a clean syringe and immediately sent to the hospital laboratory to test for aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, mycobacteria, and fungus. Although Gram staining, Ziehl-Neelsen staining, and bacterial and fungal cultures showed negative results, polymerase chain reaction for M. tuberculosis was positive. Blood culture was not performed. Four weeks later, the M. tuberculosis isolate was cultured from the pus specimen and showed good sensitivity to isoniazid, rifampicin, ethambutol, streptomycin, and levofloxacin. She was diagnosed with tuberculous mastitis and sternal osteomyelitis. A 2-month intensive phase of therapy with isoniazid (300 mg daily), rifampicin (450 mg daily), ethambutol (750 mg daily), and pyrazinamide (1200 mg daily) was initiated, followed by 7-month continuation therapy with isoniazid and rifampicin. After completion of the treatment regimen, the fistula and deformity in the breast were healed (Fig. b), and MRI findings of the lesion showed considerable improvement (Fig. b). At the 2-year follow-up after treatment, she was cured of the disease and experienced no recurrence or relapse.

args: A 69-year-old man was driven to the emergency department by his spouse late this afternoon after complaining of two days of progressively thicker yellow sputum, low-grade fever, and a sense of “heaviness” in the chest that became pronounced whenever he walked from the living room to the kitchen. He lives in a single-story ranch house, mows his own lawn with frequent rests, and normally manages his medications and finances without assistance. His longstanding medical problems include a chronically reduced left-ventricular ejection fraction from ischemic cardiomyopathy—last transthoracic echo three months ago documented an EF of 30 %—and slowly worsening chronic kidney disease attributed to decades of hypertensive nephrosclerosis; serum creatinine has hovered around 2 mg/dL at primary-care visits, and he has never undergone dialysis. The heart-failure clinic recently titrated him to carvedilol 25 mg twice daily and torsemide 40 mg each morning, and a nephrologist added low-dose phosphate binder but no erythropoietin therapy. He has never been told he has cirrhosis, cancer, or prior stroke, and he denies residence in any long-term care facility. He quit smoking fifteen years earlier after a 25-pack-year exposure, drinks a single beer with weekend dinners, and does not use illicit substances. Vaccination records on his phone confirm an inactivated influenza shot four months ago and a pneumococcal conjugate injection two years prior. His wife reports that on the drive he sat upright, conversed appropriately, and answered the day’s date correctly. They purposely avoided acetaminophen, inhalers, antibiotics, supplemental oxygen, or diuretics today so hospital staff could “see the real numbers.” On arrival the patient appeared tired but alert, speaking in full sentences with mild exertional pauses. Skin was warm, lightly diaphoretic, and without peripheral mottling; capillary refill was under two seconds. Untreated triage vitals recorded an oral temperature of 100.3 °F, regular pulse 81 beats per minute, automated blood pressure 91/54 mm Hg confirmed by repeat manual cuff, respiratory rate 25 breaths per minute with shallow excursions, and fingertip pulse oximetry 93 % while breathing ambient air at this sea-level facility. Jugular venous distention rose to about 5 cm above the sternal angle while reclined at 45°, and there was trace pitting edema to the mid-shin bilaterally—unchanged from baseline per cardiology notes. Cardiac examination revealed a soft S3 with no new murmurs or rub; the PMI was laterally displaced. Lung examination demonstrated bronchial breath sounds and coarse crackles over the right middle lobe anteriorly and the right lower posterior field, with resonant percussion throughout and no stony dullness or discrete shifting note to suggest free fluid. The abdomen was scaphoid, nontender, and without palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic screen showed equal reactive pupils, intact extra-ocular movements, clear speech, and symmetric 5/5 strength in all extremities. A bedside capillary glucose obtained before intravenous access measured 109 mg/dL. Venous blood drawn within ten minutes of arrival—prior to any saline infusion—revealed sodium 140 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 66 mg/dL, and creatinine 2.4 mg/dL. Complete blood count demonstrated leukocytes 14.2 × 10⁹/L with 89 % neutrophils, hematocrit 36 %, hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL, and platelets 210 × 10⁹/L. A right radial arterial sample obtained while he remained on room air returned pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 47 mm Hg, PaO₂ 81 mm Hg, calculated bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, and base excess –4, consistent with a mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis. Serum lactate was 2.1 mmol/L. A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph, obtained before any medication or intravenous fluid, showed a dense homogeneous consolidation occupying the right lower lobe with air bronchograms; both costophrenic angles were sharply defined, the hemidiaphragms sat at normal height, and cardiac silhouette was mildly enlarged but unchanged from an image six months earlier. No layering meniscus or blunting was visualized. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram displayed sinus rhythm at 80 beats per minute with Q waves in leads V1–V3 from prior infarction and no acute ST-T changes. Two peripheral blood-culture sets, an expectorated sputum specimen for Gram stain and culture, urinary antigens for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella, and a respiratory viral PCR panel were dispatched before any antimicrobial, bronchodilator, vasopressor, diuretic, or antipyretic was administered. Up to the completion of this note the patient has received no intravenous fluids, oxygen, or medication other than minimal heparinized saline used to lock the arterial catheter, so all physiologic observations, laboratory values, and imaging findings above represent his untreated presentation.

args: A 70-year-old woman who lives alone and is usually independent in her activities of daily living was brought to the emergency department by emergency medical services after her neighbor found her sitting on the kitchen floor, coughing violently and appearing mildly confused; the neighbor reported that the patient had complained over the prior two days of chills, productive yellow sputum, and generalized weakness but had declined to seek care because she “didn’t want to bother anyone.” The patient’s medical history includes well-controlled hyperlipidemia managed with a statin, remote cholecystectomy three decades earlier, childhood appendectomy, and diet-controlled pre-diabetes; she has never needed dialysis, has no chronic kidney disease (her most recent estimated glomerular filtration rate six months ago was in the normal range), no chronic lung disease, no liver cirrhosis, no malignancy, is HIV negative, has never undergone solid-organ or bone-marrow transplantation, and has never used corticosteroids, cytotoxic chemotherapy, or biologic immune modulators. She takes only atorvastatin 20 mg nightly and an over-the-counter multivitamin, denies tobacco for the past forty years, drinks wine socially once a week, and has never used illicit drugs. Vaccinations were reportedly up to date through last year’s influenza season, and she has no known drug allergies. On arrival to the resuscitation bay—before any intravenous fluids, antimicrobial therapy, antipyretics, or vasoactive medications had been started—she was seated upright on the stretcher with a non-rebreather mask delivering 60 % inspired oxygen that paramedics had applied after her saturations fell into the low 80s on room air during transport. Initial manual blood pressure by cuff measured 92/54 mm Hg, radial pulse was regular at 118 beats per minute, respiratory rate counted for a full minute was 30 breaths per minute with moderate use of accessory muscles, and rectal temperature registered 38.0 °C. She opened her eyes to speech, was able to state her name and location but was uncertain of the month, and followed simple commands, giving an overall Glasgow Coma Score of 14. Capillary refill was three seconds, distal skin felt cool but not mottled, and jugular venous pressure was not elevated. Cardiac auscultation revealed a normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary examination demonstrated coarse inspiratory crackles over the right lower and middle fields with diminished air entry basally; no wheezes were appreciated. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and minimally tender in the suprapubic region only with deep palpation; bowel sounds were present. There was no peripheral edema, calf tenderness, or skin rash. A radial arterial blood gas drawn ten minutes after arrival on the aforementioned oxygen concentration revealed a pH of 7.28, PaCO₂ 32 mm Hg, PaO₂ 86 mm Hg, and a calculated alveolar–arterial gradient of 264 mm Hg, confirming significant impairment of oxygen transfer. Laboratory studies sent simultaneously demonstrated serum sodium 147 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, bicarbonate 17 mmol/L, glucose 138 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 22 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.35 mg/dL—which was only slightly above her baseline of 1.2 mg/dL documented six months earlier, suggesting preserved renal function without new injury. Complete blood count showed white blood cells 14 800 /mm³ with 82 % neutrophils, hematocrit 46 %, hemoglobin 15 g/dL, and platelets 240 000 /µL. Serum lactate returned at 2.9 mmol/L, high-sensitivity troponin was negative, and international normalized ratio was 1.0. A portable chest radiograph obtained within the first fifteen minutes demonstrated a dense right-middle-lobe opacity with air bronchograms and mild blunting of the right costophrenic angle but no pneumothorax or cardiomegaly; bedside cardiac ultrasound revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle and a collapsible inferior vena cava, consistent with relative hypovolemia rather than pump failure. Electrocardiography displayed sinus tachycardia at 116 beats per minute with no ST-segment deviation or conduction delay. Urine obtained by straight catheter was cloudy, smelled pungent, and registered positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase on dipstick; the patient had not voided since early morning, and bladder scan showed less than 50 mL residual volume. She reported no dysuria or flank pain, but physical examination elicited mild costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. No antimicrobial agents, crystalloid bolus, vasopressors, antipyretics, or bronchodilators had yet been administered at the time the above vital signs, blood gases, imaging, and laboratory values were captured. Given the combination of moderate fever, tachycardia, relative hypotension, tachypnea, leukocytosis, high serum sodium, mild acidemia, elevated alveolar–arterial gradient on 60 % oxygen, and radiographic evidence of focal pulmonary consolidation, the emergency team proceeded with admission to the intensive care unit for aggressive fluid resuscitation, early empiric antibiotics directed at community-acquired pneumonia with possible septic physiology, and close monitoring for progression to frank shock or respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation.

Over the past year he has noted that mowing half his yard or climbing a full flight of stairs with groceries leaves him moderately winded and occasionally produces mild ankle puffiness by evening; an extra dose of torsemide taken on such days typically returns his baseline weight by the next morning. A 42-year-old Hispanic man with diabetes mellitus (DM) type 2 diagnosed five years ago and regularly treated with glybenclamide 5 mg once daily. Otherwise, the patient’s personal and familiar past medical history was unremarkable. He also denied consumption of alcohol, cigarettes, and illegal drugs. He presented to emergency department (ED) on 1 September 2017 with a history of malaise, headache, fever, and generalized body pain during the last 6 days. Three days before admission, he developed watery diarrhea (3 to 4 times a day), tachypnea, and confusion. During the following days, malaise and bowel movement frequency increased (up to 6 times a day). On the day of admission, the patient became drowsy, dyspneic, and looked very ill.
Physical examination: Body weight 50 kg, Body Mass Index 16.8 kg/m2, blood pressure: 60/30 mmHg, respiratory rate: 32 bpm, heart rate: 78 bpm, axillar temperature: 36 °C, SatO2: 99% (FiO2: 0.21); the patient was severely dehydrated, thyroid gland was not palpable; respiratory system: tachypnea, Kussmaul’s breathing; cardiovascular system: Sweaty mottled skin, and cold extremities with prolonged capillary refill time; neurologic system: Tremor in both hands, patellar and ankle hyperreflexia.
Lab: Hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, hematocrit 40, platelets 198,000/mm3, white blood cells 10,100/mm3, segmented neutrophils 70%, bands 1%; serum glucose 460 mg/dL, urea 115 mg/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL. Arterial blood gas analyses (ABG) are shown in . Liver function tests were normal, except for hypoalbuminemia and hypoproteinemia (3.0 g/dL and 5.18 g/dL, respectively). Urinalysis: pH 5, urine density 1030, leukocytes 2–4/field, granular casts 3+, glucose 3+, ketones 2+, and leucocyte esterase was negative.
Treatment with intravenous normal saline, potassium chloride, insulin infusion, sodium bicarbonate, norepinephrine, and empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole) was administered. After 10 h of intensive treatment, ABG did not change significantly. So, differential diagnosis was extended and complementary exams were ordered: plasmatic amylase 96 U/L (reference range: 28–100 U/L), lipase 17 U/L (13–60 U/L), TSH 0.024 μIU/L (adult reference range: 0.27–4.2 μIU/L), free-T4 2.16 ng/dL (reference range: 0.82–1.63 ng/dL), total-T3 0.18 ng/mL (reference range: 0.5–2.0 ng/mL), free-T3 0.42 pg/mL (reference range: 2.1–3.8 pg/mL); CPK-MB 101.3 U/L (reference range: 0–25 U/L), total CPK 505.7 U/L (normal: 39–308 U/L); troponin T 25.0 ng/mL (reference range: 0.12–0.6 ng/mL). Based on these results, hydrocortisone 100 mg every 8 h, methimazole 20 mg every 8 h, and Lugol’s solution 10 drops every 8 h, were added on the second day of treatment. Because of shock, beta-blocker was not administered. On the third day of treatment a good evolution was observed, norepinephrine and insulin infusion were discontinued, and NPH insulin was initiated. Based on physical examination, chest x-ray (CXR) ( and ), and progressive decrease in partial oxygen pressure () compatible with acute lung edema, intravenous furosemide 20 mg every 12 h was administered for 2 days.
Although ECG was normal, troponin determinations were repeated over the next days and persisted elevated. Echocardiography (5 September 2017) showed borderline pulmonary artery systolic pressure (35 mm Hg), severe LV systolic dysfunction (LV ejection fraction 35%), diastolic dysfunction of restrictive type and global hypokinesia ().
On the 6th day of treatment, hydrocortisone and Lugol’s solution were stopped, and methimazole was reduced by half. The patient was discharged on 7 September 2017, with almost complete recovery.
We also performed other complementary exams: Twenty-four hour-urine sodium, chloride, and potassium levels were also normal. Direct stool examination showed 0–1 leucocytes/HPF, no parasites, no blood, no erythrocytes. Urine culture and HIV serology was negative. Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies were negative. Cardiac catheterization and endomyocardial biopsy were not performed. Follow-up echocardiography at 6 months was completely normal. After the patient was discharged, no additional (blood or imaging) study was performed, other than echocardiography.
Written informed consent was obtained from the patients for publication of this article and accompanying images.

args: A 39-year-old woman sits upright on the stretcher in the pre-induction bay while final preparations are made for an exploratory laparotomy and possible small-bowel resection; the general surgeons anticipate a midline incision, hand-sewn anastomosis, and at least two hours of abdominal wall closure under inhalational anesthesia, after which she will be admitted to the surgical ward for postoperative monitoring. Three weeks earlier she underwent an urgent right hemicolectomy with primary ileotransverse colonic anastomosis for penetrating Crohn-related ulceration, recovered well enough to ambulate the corridor unassisted by postoperative day five, and was discharged on day seven tolerating a low-residue diet. Over the past 48 hours she has developed colicky periumbilical pain, bilious vomiting, and progressive abdominal distension; upright radiographs obtained in the emergency department last night demonstrated multiple air-fluid levels with stepladder appearance suggestive of adhesive obstruction, and computed tomography confirmed a transition point near the prior staple line without free air. She has never been treated for pulmonary embolism, and the only thrombotic events in her family involve her mother and brother as noted above. Medication list includes adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every other week (last dose 12 days ago), oral ferrous sulfate for post-surgical anemia, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, and as-needed ondansetron; she takes no hormonal contraception, selective estrogen-receptor modulators, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, corticosteroid tapers, or antiplatelet therapy. A 37-year-old previously healthy man presented due to fever with chills and rigors, arthralgia and myalgia for 5 days duration. Symptoms were associated with headache, photophobia and recurrent episodes of vomiting. He did not report urinary, respiratory or bowel symptoms to suggest a focal infection. His past medical and surgical history was unremarkable but he admitted to bathing in a stream 1 week prior to admission.
He was febrile without pallor or icterus and did not have any rashes or lymphadenopathy. Examination revealed a Glasgow Coma Scale of 15/15 and neck stiffness. Blood pressure and pulse rate were 120/70 mmHg and 88 beats/minute, respectively. The rest of the examination, including nervous system, was unremarkable.
Investigations revealed a neutrophil leukocytosis and a high C-reactive protein (Table ). Examination of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) revealed high protein, normal glucose and predominant polymorphonuclear cells. Blood, urine and CSF bacterial cultures and CSF viral studies were negative. He had raised serum creatinine level on admission which normalized during the next 48 h. Non-contrast computerized tomography of the brain, chest radiograph and ultrasound scan of the abdomen were normal.
The patient was managed as bacterial meningitis with intravenous ceftriaxone (2 g, twice daily) intravenous dexamethasone (8 mg, three times daily). Considering the history of high-risk exposure to possible contaminated water, serum leptospirosis microscopic agglutination test (MAT) was performed, yielding a positive titer of 1/5120. The serovar was identified as Leptospira borgpetersenii sv. Tarassovi (strain bakeri).

args: A 60-year-old man was assessed late this morning in the ambulatory anesthesia clinic three days before a planned outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy for a 1.1-cm radiolucent gallstone that has provoked three separate episodes of biliary colic during the past month despite a low-fat diet; the general surgeon anticipates four trocar sites, an operative time of under one hour, minimal blood loss, and discharge home the same evening once oral intake and ambulation are satisfactory. The patient drove himself, parked on the third level of the visitor ramp, and walked unhurriedly through two corridors to reception while conversing comfortably about postoperative activity restrictions. He reports that at home he routinely climbs the 14 steps to his bedroom without interruption, mows his quarter-acre lawn with a self-propelled mower, and carries groceries up one flight, all without chest tightness or undue dyspnea. Ten years ago he awoke with crushing substernal pressure radiating to the left shoulder; paramedics documented inferior ST-segment elevation, and emergent coronary angiography showed an occlusion in the mid-right coronary artery treated with a drug-eluting stent. He completed cardiac rehabilitation, has remained free of anginal symptoms, and follows up with cardiology annually; the most recent treadmill study performed nine months ago reached nine metabolic equivalents and revealed no inducible ischemia. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily, high-intensity atorvastatin 40 mg at bedtime, and metoprolol succinate 50 mg each morning; there have been no hospitalizations for chest pain, rhythm disturbance, or fluid overload since the index event. Hypertension was first noted at age 45 and is now controlled with lisinopril 20 mg nightly, office readings averaging 122/70 mm Hg. Fasting lipid panel last month showed LDL 74 mg/dL and triglycerides 110 mg/dL. He denies diabetes, and finger-stick checks performed during his spouse’s gestational diabetes monitoring years ago typically read in the 90s; no glucose-lowering agents, injectable or oral, are prescribed. There is no personal history of stroke, transient ischemic attack, carotid intervention, or heart-failure hospitalization, and he has never been told he has valvular disease or cardiomyopathy. Pulmonary history is limited to seasonal allergic rhinitis without wheeze or cough. Prior operations include a left inguinal hernia repair at age 30 under general anesthesia without complications. He quit smoking 18 years ago after a 15-pack-year exposure, drinks one glass of bourbon on Saturday nights, and denies recreational drug use. Family history reveals a father who underwent triple coronary bypass at 70 and a mother living with hypertension; there are no early cerebrovascular events. Review of systems today is negative for fever, chills, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower-limb swelling, palpitations, presyncope, claudication, unintentional weight change, gastrointestinal bleeding, or urinary symptoms beyond the episodic right upper-quadrant discomfort for which surgery is scheduled. After five minutes seated, vital signs in the right arm registered blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, regular pulse 62 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air, oral temperature 36.7 °C, height 175 cm, weight 86 kg, and body mass index 28 kg/m². He appeared well nourished and in no acute distress. Jugular venous pulse was not elevated at 45°, carotid upstrokes were brisk without bruit, and the apical impulse was located in the fifth interspace at the mid-clavicular line. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal S1 and S2 without gallop, click, or murmur; no friction rub was appreciated. Lungs were clear to percussion and auscultation throughout. The abdomen was soft with mild tenderness beneath the right costal margin on deep inspiration but no rebound, guarding, or palpable mass; Murphy sign was reproduced only with firm pressure. Extremities were warm with 2+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally and no edema or cyanosis. Neurologic screening demonstrated full orientation, intact cranial nerves, symmetric 5/5 strength, normal sensation to light touch and vibration, and a steady heel-to-toe gait. Laboratory studies drawn one hour prior to examination showed hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, hematocrit 43 %, platelet count 245 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.14 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate above 60 mL/min/1.73 m²; fasting plasma glucose measured simultaneously was 104 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 26 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 30 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 84 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, and international normalized ratio 1.0. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram obtained in clinic demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 60 beats per minute, Q waves in leads III and aVF consistent with the remote infarct, and no acute ST-segment deviations compared with a tracing from last year. Upright postero-anterior chest radiography displayed clear lung fields and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Anesthesia airway assessment documented a Mallampati class II view, inter-incisor distance 4 cm, thyromental distance 7 cm, and full cervical extension; no loose teeth were noted. The peri-operative plan is to continue beta-blocker, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor, antiplatelet therapy, and statin through the morning of surgery, permit clear liquids up to two hours before arrival, employ balanced general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation, administer cephalosporin prophylaxis after induction, and use multimodal analgesia including acetaminophen and a low-dose intravenous opioid to minimize nausea. Hemodynamic monitoring will consist of non-invasive blood pressure cycling every three minutes, and crystalloid administration will be restricted to 2 mL/kg/h given his stable renal function. Sequential compression sleeves will be applied in the operating room, and early ambulation with oral hydration is anticipated four hours post-procedure. After discussing potential but unlikely cardiac events, pulmonary complications, bile-duct injury, infection, and the need for possible conversion to an open approach, the patient accurately repeated medication timing, fasting instructions, signs of postoperative concern, and voiced readiness to proceed with definitive gallbladder removal.
